narrative:
"Next, with the help of the Delphian oracle, we have to institute festivals and make laws about them, and to determine what sacrifices will be for the good of the city, and to what Gods they shall be offered; but when they shall be offered, and how often, may be partly regulated by us."

"The number -- yes."
refashioned:
"Our next task is to establish festivals with the guidance of the Delphic oracle and to create laws governing them. We must determine which sacrifices will benefit the city and to which gods they should be offered. However, the timing and frequency of these sacrifices -- that much we can decide ourselves."

"The number of them -- yes, that we can settle."
elegant:
"We must next establish festivals and sacrificial laws with the oracle's guidance, deciding which gods receive offerings. The timing and frequency, however, we may determine ourselves."

"The number -- yes."
summary: The Athenian proposes that while the Delphic oracle should guide which gods receive sacrifices and what form festivals take, the practical scheduling details can be determined by the legislators themselves. Cleinias agrees regarding the number.
distilled: The oracle determines which gods receive sacrifices, but the legislators themselves may regulate the timing and frequency of festivals.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The deference to the Delphic oracle reflects Plato's consistent respect for traditional religious authority in legislative matters -- cf. Republic 427b-c, where Socrates similarly defers to Apollo at Delphi for decisions about temples, sacrifices, and worship. The distinction between divine authority (which gods) and human regulation (when and how often) establishes the cooperative framework between religion and legislation that structures Book 8.

narrative:
"Then we will first determine the number; and let the whole number be 365 -- one for every day -- so that one magistrate at least will sacrifice daily to some God or demi-god on behalf of the city, and the citizens, and their possessions. And the interpreters, and priests, and priestesses, and prophets shall meet, and, in company with the guardians of the law, ordain those things which the legislator of necessity omits; and I may remark that they are the very persons who ought to take note of what is omitted.

The law will say that there are twelve feasts dedicated to the twelve Gods, after whom the several tribes are named; and that to each of them they shall sacrifice every month, and appoint choruses, and musical and gymnastic contests, assigning them so as to suit the Gods and seasons of the year. And they shall have festivals for women, distinguishing those which ought to be separated from the men's festivals, and those which ought not.

Further, they shall not confuse the infernal deities and their rites with the Gods who are termed heavenly and their rites, but shall separate them, giving to Pluto his own in the twelfth month, which is sacred to him, according to the law. To such a deity warlike men should entertain no aversion, but they should honour him as being always the best friend of man. For the connection of soul and body is no way better than the dissolution of them, as I am ready to maintain quite seriously.

Moreover, those who would regulate these matters rightly should consider, that our city among existing cities has fellow, either in respect of leisure or command of the necessaries of life, and that like an individual she ought to live happily. And those who would live happily should in the first place do no wrong to one another, and ought not themselves to be wronged by others; to attain the first is not difficult, but there is great difficulty in acquiring the power of not being wronged. No man can be perfectly secure against wrong, unless he has become perfectly good; and cities are like individuals in this, for a city if good has a life of peace, but if evil, a life of war within and without."
refashioned:
"Let us begin by fixing the total number of festivals at 365 -- one for each day of the year -- ensuring that at least one magistrate performs a daily sacrifice to some god or demigod on behalf of the city, its citizens, and their property. The interpreters, priests, priestesses, and prophets shall convene together with the guardians of the law to establish whatever the legislator necessarily leaves unaddressed. And indeed, these are precisely the people who should be attentive to such gaps.

The law shall prescribe twelve major feasts dedicated to the twelve gods for whom the tribes are named. Each month shall include sacrifices to the appropriate god, along with choruses and contests in music and athletics, arranged to suit both the gods honored and the seasons of the year. There shall also be festivals specifically for women, with clear distinctions between those that should be held separately from men's festivals and those that need not be.

Furthermore, the rites of the underworld deities must not be confused with those of the heavenly gods. They must be kept apart. Pluto shall have his own observances in the twelfth month, which is sacred to him by law. Warriors should feel no hostility toward such a deity; rather, they should honor him as humanity's truest friend. For I am prepared to argue in all seriousness that the union of soul and body is in no way superior to their separation.

Those who would order these matters properly must also recognize that our city, among all existing cities, is unrivaled in both leisure and command of life's necessities, and that, like an individual, it ought to live happily. Those who wish to live happily must first refrain from wronging one another and must also not be wronged by others. The first condition is not hard to achieve, but the second -- acquiring the power to avoid being wronged -- is profoundly difficult. No one can be perfectly secure against injustice unless he has become perfectly good. Cities mirror individuals in this respect: a good city enjoys peace, while an evil one endures war both within and without."
elegant:
"Let there be 365 festivals -- one daily sacrifice by a magistrate on behalf of the city. Priests, interpreters, and law-guardians shall jointly fill whatever gaps the legislator leaves. Twelve major feasts shall honor the twelve tribal gods monthly, with choruses and contests suited to the seasons. Women shall have their own festivals. The rites of the underworld gods must be kept separate from those of the heavenly gods, with Pluto honored in the twelfth month. Warriors should not despise him; death is the soul's truest friend, for the union of body and soul is no better than their dissolution.

Our city, blessed with leisure and abundance, ought to live happily. Happiness requires both doing no wrong and suffering none. The first is easy; the second is nearly impossible without perfect goodness. A good city lives in peace; an evil city lives in perpetual war."
summary: The Athenian establishes a comprehensive festival calendar of 365 daily sacrifices, twelve major feasts for the tribal gods, and separate observances for women and for the underworld deities. He argues that Pluto deserves honor rather than aversion, since the separation of soul from body is no worse than their union. He concludes that a city blessed with leisure must pursue goodness to achieve peace, since only perfect virtue can protect against being wronged.
distilled: A city should hold daily sacrifices, honor both heavenly and underworld gods in proper separation, and recognize that only perfect goodness can secure happiness and peace.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The claim that the dissolution of soul and body is no worse than their union echoes the Phaedo's argument that death is the philosopher's liberation (Phaedo 64a-67b). The twelve tribal gods correspond to the twelve Olympians -- cf. Laws 745d-e and 771c for the tribal organization. The principle that security from injustice requires perfect goodness parallels Republic 335b-e on justice. The emphasis on Pluto as 'the best friend of man' is a striking revaluation that connects to the Cratylus 403a-404a, where Plato etymologizes Pluto's name as 'wealth-giver.' The distinction between heavenly and chthonic rites reflects standard Greek religious practice.

narrative:
"Wherefore the citizens ought to practise war -- not in time of war, but rather while they are at peace. And every city which has any sense, should take the field at least for one day in every month; and for more if the magistrates think fit, having no regard to winter cold or summer heat; and they should go out en masse, including their wives and their children, when the magistrates determine to lead forth the whole people, or in separate portions when summoned by them; and they should always provide that there should be games and sacrificial feasts, and they should have tournaments, imitating in as lively a manner as they can real battles.

And they should distribute prizes of victory and valour to the competitors, passing censures and encomiums on one another according to the characters which they bear in the contests and their whole life, honouring him who seems to be the best, and blaming him who is the opposite.

And let poets celebrate the victors -- not however every poet, but only one who in the first place is not less than fifty years of age; nor should he be one who, although he may have musical and poetical gifts, has never in his life done any noble or illustrious action; but those who are themselves good and also honourable in the state, creators of noble actions -- let their poems be sung, even though they be not very musical.

And let the judgment of them rest with the instructor of youth and the other guardians of the laws, who shall give them this privilege, and they alone shall be free to sing; but the rest of the world shall not have this liberty. Nor shall any one dare to sing a song which has not been approved by the judgment of the guardians of the laws, not even if his strain be sweeter than the songs of Thamyras and Orpheus; but only such poems as have been judged sacred and dedicated to the Gods, and such as are the works of good men, which praise or blame has been awarded and which have been deemed to fulfil their design fairly."
refashioned:
"Therefore, citizens should train for war not during wartime but during peace. Any city with good sense should conduct field exercises at least one day each month -- more if the magistrates see fit -- regardless of winter cold or summer heat. The entire population, including wives and children, should march out when the magistrates call for a general mobilization, or in smaller groups when summoned. These outings should always include games and sacrificial feasts, with tournaments that simulate actual combat as realistically as possible.

Prizes for victory and valor should be awarded to the competitors. Citizens should publicly praise and censure one another based on their conduct both in these contests and in their lives generally, honoring those who prove best and criticizing those who fall short.

As for poetry celebrating the victors: not just any poet should compose it. The poet must be at least fifty years old, and he must not be someone who possesses literary talent but has never performed a noble deed in his own life. Rather, let those who are themselves virtuous and respected in the state -- people who have accomplished great things -- have their poems sung, even if their verse is not particularly polished.

The director of education and the guardians of the law shall judge which poems qualify, and only approved poets shall have the freedom to perform publicly. No one else may sing publicly, even if his music surpasses the songs of Thamyras and Orpheus. Only poems judged sacred and dedicated to the gods, or works by good citizens that have been evaluated for their proper use of praise and blame, shall be permitted."
elegant:
"Citizens should train for war during peacetime. Every sensible city should hold military exercises monthly, in all weather, with the whole population -- wives and children included. These exercises should feature realistic combat simulations, games, feasts, and prizes for valor, with public praise for the best and censure for the worst.

Only poets over fifty who have themselves performed noble deeds may celebrate the victors. The guardians of the law shall approve all public poetry. No song may be performed without their sanction, however beautiful -- only works that are sacred, composed by good citizens, and properly employ praise and blame."
summary: The Athenian argues that citizens should practice warfare during peacetime through monthly military exercises involving the entire population, with realistic combat simulations, prizes, and public evaluation of character. He restricts the poetry celebrating victors to elder poets of proven virtue, subject to state approval, even if less skilled poets produce more beautiful verse.
distilled: Citizens must train for war during peace through monthly exercises with the whole population, and only virtuous elder poets approved by the state may celebrate the victors.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The insistence on peacetime military training anticipates the Roman concept of training as preparation (cf. Vegetius). The age requirement for poets (50+) and the demand for personal virtue echo Republic 378-403 on censorship of poetry, though here the mechanism is institutional approval rather than outright prohibition. Thamyras was a mythical Thracian singer who challenged the Muses and was blinded -- mentioned also at Iliad 2.594-600. The integration of wives and children into military exercises reflects the Laws' more inclusive social vision compared to earlier dialogues.

narrative:
"The regulations about military training and about liberty of speech in poetry ought to apply equally to men and women. The legislator may be supposed to argue the question in his own mind: 'Who are my citizens for whom I have set in order the city? Are they not competitors in the greatest of all contests, and have they not innumerable rivals?'

To be sure, will be the natural reply.

'Well, but if we were training boxers, or pancratiasts, or any other sort of athletes, would they never meet until the hour of contest arrived; and should we do nothing to prepare ourselves previously by daily practice? Surely, if we were boxers we should have been learning to fight for many days before, and exercising ourselves in imitating all those blows and wards which we were intending to use in the hour of conflict; and in order that we might come as near to reality as possible, instead of cestuses we should put on boxing gloves, that the blows and the wards might be practised by us to the utmost of our power. And if there were a lack of competitors, the ridicule of fools would not deter us from hanging up a lifeless image and practising at that. Or if we had no adversary at all, animate or inanimate, should we not venture in the dearth of antagonists to spar by ourselves? In what other manner could we ever study the art of self-defence?'"

"The way which you mention, Stranger, would be the only way."
refashioned:
"These regulations regarding military training and freedom of expression in poetry should apply equally to men and women. The legislator should reason with himself as follows: 'Who are the citizens for whom I am building this city? Are they not contestants in the greatest of all competitions, facing countless rivals?'

The natural answer is: of course they are.

'Then consider: if we were training boxers or mixed martial artists or athletes of any kind, would we let them sit idle until the day of competition arrived, doing nothing to prepare through daily practice? Of course not. If we were boxers, we would spend many days beforehand learning to fight, rehearsing every punch and defensive move we planned to use in actual combat. To make our practice as realistic as possible, we would train with padded gloves instead of bare fists so we could drill at full intensity. If we lacked sparring partners, we would not let mockery stop us from setting up a punching bag and training against that. And if we had no target at all -- living or otherwise -- we would shadowbox alone rather than neglect our training. How else could we ever learn self-defense?'"

"The method you describe, Stranger, would be the only way."
elegant:
"Military and poetic regulations should apply equally to men and women. The legislator should ask: 'My citizens face the greatest contest of all -- life itself. Would any athlete skip daily training and wait for competition day? A boxer drills punches on gloves, bags, or even thin air. Why should citizens preparing for the ultimate contest do any less?'"

"That would be the only way, Stranger."
summary: The Athenian argues that military and poetic regulations should apply equally to both sexes, then uses the analogy of athletic training to show that citizens must practice constantly for the contest of life itself. Just as boxers would never wait until competition day to begin training -- even practicing on dummies or shadowboxing if no partners were available -- so citizens must drill for their civic duties.
distilled: Citizens of both sexes must train constantly for the great contest of life, just as athletes practice daily with whatever means are available.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The equal application of regulations to women is a distinctive feature of the Laws, paralleling Republic 451c-457b on women's education. The pancration (pankration) was an ancient Greek combat sport combining wrestling and boxing. Cestuses were leather hand-wraps reinforced with metal, used in ancient boxing. The boxing analogy illustrates the broader Platonic principle that virtue requires habitual practice -- cf. Aristotle's later development of this idea in Nicomachean Ethics 1103a-b, which may have been influenced by this passage.

narrative:
"And shall the warriors of our city, who are destined when occasion calls to enter the greatest of all contests, and to fight for their lives, and their children, and their property, and the whole city, be worse prepared than boxers? And will the legislator, because he is afraid that their practising with one another may appear to some ridiculous, abstain from commanding them to go out and fight; will he not ordain that soldiers shall perform lesser exercises without arms every day, making dancing and all gymnastic tend to this end; and also will he not require that they shall practise some gymnastic exercises, greater as well as lesser, as often as every month; and that they shall have contests one with another in every part of the country, seizing upon posts and lying in ambush, and imitating in every respect the reality of war; fighting with boxing-gloves and hurling javelins, and using weapons somewhat dangerous, and as nearly as possible like the true ones, in order that the sport may not be altogether without fear, but may have terrors and to a certain degree show the man who has and who has not courage; and that the honour and dishonour which are assigned to them respectively, may prepare the whole city for the true conflict of life?

If any one dies in these mimic contests, the homicide is involuntary, and we will make the slayer, when he has been purified according to law, to be pure of blood, considering that if a few men should die, others as good as they will be born; but that if fear is dead then the citizens will never find a test of superior and inferior natures, which is a far greater evil to the state than the loss of a few."
refashioned:
"Should the warriors of our city -- who may at any moment be called upon to fight for their lives, their families, their property, and the city itself -- be less prepared than boxers? Should the legislator, out of fear that military drills might look ridiculous to some, refrain from ordering citizens to train for combat? Surely not.

He should require soldiers to perform lighter exercises without weapons every day, directing all dance and gymnastics toward military readiness. At least once a month, they should engage in more intensive exercises, holding contests throughout the countryside: capturing strategic positions, setting ambushes, and simulating every aspect of real warfare. They should fight with padded gloves, throw javelins, and use weapons that carry some genuine danger -- close approximations of real arms -- so that the training produces real fear and genuinely reveals who possesses courage and who does not. The honor and dishonor distributed through these exercises will prepare the entire city for the true struggles of life.

If someone dies in these simulated battles, the killing is involuntary. Once the killer has been purified according to law, he shall be considered clean of bloodguilt. We must recognize that while a few may die, others equally capable will be born to replace them. But if fear itself dies -- if the element of danger is removed from training -- then citizens will never have a way to distinguish superior natures from inferior ones. That loss would be a far greater evil to the state than the death of a few individuals."
elegant:
"Should our warriors be worse prepared than boxers? The legislator must order daily exercises, monthly combat drills, and realistic war games throughout the countryside -- with real danger, real ambushes, and weapons close to the genuine article. Training must produce genuine fear, because only fear reveals who has courage and who lacks it.

If someone dies in these exercises, the killing is involuntary and the purified slayer is absolved. A few deaths are replaceable; but the death of fear in training -- the inability to distinguish the courageous from the cowardly -- is an incomparably greater loss to the state."
summary: The Athenian argues that military training must involve real danger to be effective, including daily exercises, monthly combat drills, and realistic war simulations with near-real weapons. He accepts that some training deaths are inevitable but argues they are involuntary homicides that pale in comparison to the greater evil of removing fear from training, which would prevent the state from identifying its bravest citizens.
distilled: Military training must involve genuine danger because the inability to test courage is a far greater evil to the state than the occasional training death.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The acceptance of training deaths as involuntary homicide connects to the Laws' detailed treatment of homicide categories in Book 9 (865a-874d). The purification ritual for involuntary killing reflects actual Greek legal and religious practice. The argument that danger is essential to training anticipates Aristotle's point that courage requires exposure to frightening situations (Nicomachean Ethics 1104a). The distinction between 'superior and inferior natures' echoes Republic 415a-c (the myth of metals). The subordination of individual life to civic good is characteristic of Plato's political philosophy throughout the Republic and Laws.

narrative:
"We are quite agreed, Stranger, that we should legislate about such things, and that the whole state should practise them."

"And what is the reason that dances and contests of this sort hardly ever exist in states, at least not to any extent worth speaking of? Is this due to the ignorance of mankind and their legislators?"

"Perhaps."

"Certainly not, sweet Cleinias; there are two causes, which are quite enough to account for the deficiency."

"What are they?"
refashioned:
"We fully agree, Stranger, that we should create laws governing these matters and that the entire state should participate in such training."

"Then why is it that military dances and contests of this kind almost never exist in actual states -- at least not to any meaningful degree? Is this simply due to ignorance on the part of the people and their lawmakers?"

"Perhaps it is."

"Not at all, my dear Cleinias. There are two specific causes that fully explain why this is lacking."

"What are they?"
elegant:
Cleinias agrees the whole state should practice military exercises. The Athenian asks why such training is so rare, and rejects ignorance as the explanation -- there are two specific causes.

"What are they?"
summary: After Cleinias confirms agreement on military training legislation, the Athenian raises the question of why such beneficial practices are so rare in actual states. He dismisses ignorance as the explanation and promises to identify two specific root causes.
distilled: The rarity of military training in states is not due to ignorance but to two specific causes the Athenian is about to identify.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. The affectionate address 'sweet Cleinias' reflects the conversational warmth characteristic of Plato's later dialogues. The transition from agreement to diagnosis of political obstacles is a typical Platonic move -- identifying structural rather than merely intellectual barriers to good governance. The 'two causes' (love of wealth and bad government) will be elaborated in 8.7-8.9, forming a political analysis that parallels the classification of defective regimes in Republic 8.

narrative:
"One cause is the love of wealth, which wholly absorbs men, and never for a moment allows them to think of anything but their own private possessions; on this the soul of every citizen hangs suspended, and can attend to nothing but his daily gain; mankind are ready to learn any branch of knowledge, and to follow any pursuit which tends to this end, and they laugh at every other -- that is one reason why a city will not be in earnest about such contests or any other good and honourable pursuit.

But from an insatiable love of gold and silver, every man will stoop to any art or contrivance, seemly or unseemly, in the hope of becoming rich; and will make no objection to performing any action, holy, or unholy and utterly base, if only like a beast he have the power of eating and drinking all kinds of things, and procuring for himself in every sort of way the gratification of his lusts."
refashioned:
"The first cause is the love of wealth. It consumes people entirely, never allowing them a moment's thought for anything beyond their private possessions. Every citizen's soul is suspended from this single hook, fixated on daily profit. People are eager to learn any skill or pursue any activity that promises financial gain, and they mock everything else. This is one reason why cities never take seriously military contests or any other noble and worthwhile pursuit.

Driven by an insatiable craving for gold and silver, every person will resort to any means -- respectable or disgraceful -- in the hope of getting rich. They will carry out any deed, whether sacred or profane and utterly degrading, so long as it enables them, like animals, to gorge themselves on food and drink and to satisfy their appetites in every conceivable way."
elegant: "The first cause is the love of wealth. It monopolizes every citizen's attention, leaving no room for anything beyond private gain. People eagerly learn whatever makes money and ridicule everything else. Driven by insatiable greed for gold and silver, they will stoop to any act -- sacred or base -- to get rich, reducing themselves to beasts concerned only with appetite."
summary: The Athenian identifies the love of wealth as the first cause preventing cities from taking military training seriously. This obsession with private gain absorbs citizens so completely that they ridicule all pursuits that do not generate profit, and their insatiable greed drives them to any means -- noble or base -- to accumulate wealth and satisfy bodily appetites.
distilled: The love of wealth so completely absorbs citizens that they ridicule all noble pursuits and will stoop to any means to satisfy their greed.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The critique of wealth-obsession as reducing humans to beasts parallels Republic 571c-572b on the tyrannical soul enslaved to appetites. The image of the soul 'hanging suspended' on gain is vivid and original to this passage. The dismissal of profit-seeking as incompatible with civic virtue connects to Laws 5.743d-e, which limits property accumulation. Plato's anti-commercial stance here also echoes Republic 373d-e on the 'feverish' city and its expansion through luxury. The beast metaphor recurs throughout Plato -- cf. Republic 588c-589b on the many-headed beast within the soul.

narrative:
"True."

"Let this, then, be deemed one of the causes which prevent states from pursuing in an efficient manner the art of war, or any other noble aim, but makes the orderly and temperate part of mankind into merchants, and captains of ships, and servants, and converts the valiant sort into thieves and burglars and robbers of temples, and violent, tyrannical persons; many of whom are not without ability, but they are unfortunate."

"What do you mean?"

"Must not they be truly unfortunate whose souls are compelled to pass through life always hungering?"

"Then that is one cause, Stranger; but you spoke of another."

"Thank you for reminding me."

"The insatiable lifelong love of wealth, as you were saying, is one cause which absorbs mankind, and prevents them from rightly practising the arts of war -- Granted; and now tell me, what is the other?"

"Do you imagine that I delay because I am in a perplexity?"

"No; but we think that you are too severe upon the money-loving temper, of which you seem in the present discussion to have a peculiar dislike."

"That is a very fair rebuke, Cleinias; and I will now proceed to the second cause."

"Proceed."
refashioned:
"True enough."

"So let us recognize this as one of the causes that prevent states from effectively pursuing the art of war or any other noble aim. The love of wealth takes the orderly and moderate citizens and turns them into merchants, ship captains, and servants. It takes the bold and spirited and turns them into thieves, burglars, temple robbers, and violent tyrants. Many of these people are not without talent -- but they are deeply unfortunate."

"What do you mean by unfortunate?"

"Can anyone whose soul is condemned to pass through life in a state of perpetual hunger be called anything other than unfortunate?"

"That is one cause, then, Stranger. But you mentioned a second."

"Thank you for the reminder."

"You've established that the insatiable, lifelong love of wealth absorbs people and prevents them from properly cultivating the arts of war. Granted. Now tell us -- what is the second cause?"

"Do you think I'm hesitating because I'm confused?"

"No, but it seems to us that you have a particular hostility toward the love of money, which you've been attacking rather severely in this discussion."

"That is a perfectly fair criticism, Cleinias. I will now move on to the second cause."

"Please do."
elegant:
The love of wealth corrupts citizens in two ways: it turns the moderate into mere merchants and servants, and it turns the bold into criminals and tyrants. These people are not untalented -- they are unfortunate, their souls condemned to perpetual hunger.

Cleinias gently accuses the Athenian of excessive hostility toward wealth. The Athenian accepts the rebuke and promises to address the second cause.
summary: The Athenian explains how wealth-obsession corrupts both moderate and bold citizens, turning the former into merchants and the latter into criminals. He calls them unfortunate because their souls hunger endlessly. Cleinias gently criticizes the Athenian's apparent fixation on condemning wealth, which the Athenian gracefully accepts before moving to the second cause.
distilled: The love of wealth corrupts moderate citizens into merchants and bold citizens into criminals, leaving their souls in perpetual hunger.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. The observation that capable but corrupted people become tyrants and criminals rather than good citizens parallels Republic 495a-496a, where Socrates notes that the most gifted natures, if badly raised, produce the greatest evils. The metaphor of the soul 'always hungering' connects to the Gorgias 493a-494a (the leaky jar metaphor for insatiable desire). Cleinias's rebuke introduces a moment of dramatic self-awareness rare in Platonic dialogue -- the interlocutor pushes back on the main speaker's apparent bias. The distinction between orderly/temperate and valiant natures reflects the Republic's division of the soul into rational, spirited, and appetitive parts (435b-441c).

narrative:
"I say that governments are a cause -- democracy, oligarchy, tyranny, concerning which I have often spoken in the previous discourse; or rather governments they are not, for none of them exercises a voluntary rule over voluntary subjects; but they may be truly called states of discord, in which while the government is voluntary, the subjects always obey against their will, and have to be coerced; and the ruler fears the subject, and will not, if he can help, allow him to become either noble, or rich, or strong, or valiant, or warlike at all.

These two are the chief causes of almost all evils, and of the evils of which I have been speaking they are notably the causes. But our state has escaped both of them; for her citizens have the greatest leisure, and they are not subject to one another, and will, I think, be made by these laws the reverse of lovers of money. Such a constitution may be reasonably supposed to be the only one existing which will accept the education which we have described, and the martial pastimes which have been perfected according to our idea."
refashioned:
"The second cause is government itself -- democracy, oligarchy, tyranny -- forms I have discussed at length before. Or rather, these are not truly governments at all, since none of them involves willing rulers governing willing subjects. They should be called states of faction and discord. In each of them, the rulers hold power willingly enough, but the subjects obey only under compulsion. The ruler, fearing the subject, will do everything in his power to prevent him from becoming noble, wealthy, strong, courageous, or militarily capable.

These two causes -- the love of wealth and corrupt forms of government -- are responsible for nearly all political evils, and especially for the ones we have been discussing. But our proposed state escapes both. Its citizens will enjoy the greatest leisure. They will not be subjected to one another's domination. And these laws, I believe, will make them the very opposite of money-lovers. This may well be the only constitution in existence that can genuinely embrace the education we have described and the military training we have designed."
elegant:
"The second cause is corrupt government -- democracy, oligarchy, tyranny. These are not true governments but states of discord, where rulers govern willing but subjects obey unwillingly. Rulers fear their subjects and prevent them from becoming noble, strong, or warlike.

Our state escapes both causes: its citizens enjoy leisure, are free from domination, and will be cured of money-love. It may be the only constitution capable of accepting our education and military training."
summary: The Athenian identifies corrupt government as the second cause preventing military training, arguing that democracy, oligarchy, and tyranny are really states of discord where rulers deliberately suppress the development of their subjects. The proposed city escapes both causes -- wealth-obsession and bad government -- making it uniquely suited to implement the education and military training they have designed.
distilled: Corrupt governments suppress citizens' development out of fear, but the proposed state, free from both wealth-obsession and factional rule, is uniquely capable of true education.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The critique of democracy, oligarchy, and tyranny as 'not really governments' echoes the Statesman 291d-303b, where only the rule of the true statesman qualifies as genuine government. The idea that these regimes are 'states of discord' (staseis) rather than constitutions (politeiai) parallels Republic 545c-576b's degeneration of regimes. The ruler's fear of capable subjects is especially applicable to tyranny -- cf. Republic 567b-568a. The claim that the proposed state uniquely escapes these defects reflects the Laws' aspiration to be a 'second-best' but practically achievable ideal (Laws 739a-e), in contrast to the Republic's philosopher-king model.

narrative:
"True."

"Then next we must remember, about all gymnastic contests, that only the warlike sort of them are to be practised and to have prizes of victory; and those which are not military are to be given up. The military sort had better be completely described and established by law; and first, let us speak of running and swiftness."

"Very good."

"Certainly the most military of all qualities is general activity of body, whether of foot or hand. For escaping or for capturing an enemy, quickness of foot is required; but hand-to-hand conflict and combat need vigour and strength."

"Very true."

"Neither of them can attain their greatest efficiency without arms."

"How can they?"
refashioned:
"True."

"Then let us establish this principle regarding all gymnastic contests: only those with military value should be practiced and awarded prizes. Non-military athletics should be abandoned. We should thoroughly define and legislate the military exercises, beginning with running and speed."

"Very good."

"The most militarily useful quality is overall physical agility -- of both foot and hand. Speed of foot is essential for pursuing or escaping an enemy, while close combat demands power and strength."

"Very true."

"But neither running nor fighting reaches its full effectiveness without arms and armor."

"How could it?"
elegant:
Only military gymnastics deserve prizes; non-military athletics should be abandoned. The most militarily valuable quality is overall physical agility. Speed is needed for pursuit and escape; strength for close combat. Neither reaches full effectiveness without arms.

"How could it?"
summary: The Athenian establishes that only military-oriented gymnastic contests should be practiced and rewarded, while non-military athletics should be discontinued. He identifies overall physical agility as the most important military quality, noting that speed serves pursuit and escape while strength serves combat, but neither is fully effective without weapons training.
distilled: Only military athletics deserve public support, since the most important physical qualities -- speed and strength -- reach full effectiveness only when combined with weapons training.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. The subordination of athletics to military training contrasts with the Panhellenic festival culture of Plato's time, where athletic contests (Olympic, Pythian, Nemean, Isthmian) were celebrated for their own sake. This represents a characteristically Spartan attitude -- cf. Plutarch, Life of Lycurgus 19-22 on Spartan physical training. The emphasis on armed running anticipates the specific race descriptions in 8.11. The principle that athletics should serve civic and military purposes rather than individual glory is consistent with Republic 403c-412a on the proper role of gymnastics in education.

narrative:
"Then our herald, in accordance with the prevailing practice, will first summon the runner; -- he will appear armed, for to an unarmed competitor we will not give a prize. And he shall enter first who is to run the single course bearing arms; next, he who is to run the double course; third, he who is to run the horse-course; and fourthly, he who is to run the long course; the fifth whom we start, shall be the first sent forth in heavy armour, and shall run a course of sixty stadia to some temple of Ares -- and we will send forth another, whom we will style the more heavily armed, to run over smoother ground.

There remains the archer; and he shall run in the full equipments of an archer a distance of 100 stadia over mountains, and across every sort of country, to a temple of Apollo and Artemis; this shall be the order of the contest, and we will wait for them until they return, and will give a prize to the conqueror in each."

"Very good."
refashioned:
"Our herald, following established practice, will first call the runners -- and every competitor must appear armed, since we award no prizes to the unarmed. The first to race will run the single course in arms; the second, the double course; the third, the horse-course distance; and the fourth, the long course. The fifth competitor will be the first to race in heavy armor, running sixty stadia to a temple of Ares. A sixth runner, whom we will call 'the more heavily armed,' will cover the same distance over smoother terrain.

Finally, the archer: wearing full archery equipment, he will run one hundred stadia across mountains and rough terrain to a temple of Apollo and Artemis. This shall be the order of events. We will wait for each competitor to return and award a prize to the winner of every race."

"Very good."
elegant:
"The herald summons armed runners only -- no prizes for the unarmed. Races proceed from the single course to the double, horse-course, and long course, all in arms. Heavy-armored runners race sixty stadia to a temple of Ares. The archer, fully equipped, runs one hundred stadia over mountains to a temple of Apollo and Artemis. Winners of each race receive prizes."

"Very good."
summary: The Athenian prescribes a series of armed foot races of increasing length and difficulty, from a single course to a 100-stadia mountain run for archers. All competitors must carry weapons or armor, with heavy-armed runners directed toward a temple of Ares and archers toward a temple of Apollo and Artemis. Prizes go to each race's winner.
distilled: All foot races must be run in arms or armor, progressing from short courses to a grueling 100-stadia mountain run for archers, with prizes awarded in each event.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. A stadion (singular of stadia) was approximately 185 meters, making 60 stadia roughly 11 kilometers and 100 stadia roughly 18.5 kilometers. The dedication of the heavy-armed race to Ares (god of war) and the archery race to Apollo and Artemis (gods of the bow) reflects the Greek practice of associating athletic contests with divine patronage. Armed races (hoplitodromos) were an actual event at the ancient Olympic Games, added in 520 BCE. The increasing difficulty of the courses -- from track to mountains -- mirrors the escalating demands of actual military campaigns.

narrative:
"Let us suppose that there are three kinds of contests -- one of boys, another of beardless youths, and a third of men. For the youths we will fix the length of the contest at two-thirds, and for the boys at half of the entire course, whether they contend as archers or as heavy armed.

Touching the women, let the girls who are not grown up compete naked in the stadium and the double course, and the horse-course and the long course, and let them run on the race-ground itself; those who are thirteen years of age and upwards until their marriage shall continue to share in contests if they are not more than twenty, and shall be compelled to run up to eighteen; and they shall descend into the arena in suitable dresses. Let these be the regulations about contests in running both for men and women."
refashioned:
"Let us establish three age categories for competition: boys, beardless youths, and adult men. The youths shall race two-thirds of the full distance, and the boys shall race half, whether they compete as archers or in heavy armor.

As for women: girls who have not yet reached maturity shall compete unclothed in the stadium race, the double course, the horse-course distance, and the long course, running on the race-ground itself. Those aged thirteen and older, up until their marriage, shall continue to participate in contests provided they are no older than twenty; participation shall be mandatory through age eighteen. They shall enter the arena in appropriate clothing. These shall be our regulations for running contests for both men and women."
elegant: "Three age divisions shall compete: boys at half distance, youths at two-thirds, and men at full distance, whether as archers or heavy-armed. Young girls compete unclothed; from age thirteen to marriage (no later than twenty), women compete in appropriate dress, with participation compulsory through eighteen."
summary: The Athenian establishes three age divisions for athletic competition -- boys, youths, and men -- with proportionally shorter courses for younger competitors. Women are also included: young girls compete unclothed, while those from thirteen to twenty (or marriage) compete in suitable dress, with participation mandatory through age eighteen.
distilled: Athletic contests are divided into three male age groups with proportional distances, and women from girlhood through age twenty participate under their own regulations.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The inclusion of women in athletic competition is one of the most radical proposals in the Laws, paralleling Republic 452a-457b where Socrates argues women should exercise naked alongside men. The Spartan practice of female athletic training (Plutarch, Life of Lycurgus 14) provides historical precedent. The age categories -- boys, beardless youths (roughly 14-18), and men -- mirror the actual divisions at Panhellenic games. The compulsory participation of women through eighteen reflects the Laws' consistent theme that the state has a legitimate interest in citizens' physical development.

narrative:
"Respecting contests of strength, instead of wrestling and similar contests of the heavier sort, we will institute conflicts in armour of one against one, and two against two, and so on up to ten against ten. As to what a man ought not to suffer or do, and to what extent, in order to gain the victory -- as in wrestling, the masters of the art have laid down what is fair and what is not fair, so in fighting in armour -- we ought to call in skilful persons, who shall judge for us and be our assessors in the work of legislation; they shall say who deserves to be victor in combats of this sort, and what he is not to do or have done to him, and in like manner what rule determines who is defeated; and let these ordinances apply to women until they married as well as to men.

The pancration shall have a counterpart in a combat of the light armed; they shall contend with bows and with light shields and with javelins and in the throwing of stones by slings and by hand: and laws shall be made about it, and rewards and prizes given to him who best fulfils the ordinances of the law."
refashioned:
"For contests of strength, we will replace wrestling and similar heavy combat sports with armored fighting: one against one, two against two, and scaling up to ten against ten. Just as wrestling masters have established rules for what is fair and foul, we need skilled experts to serve as our advisors in legislating armored combat. They will determine who qualifies as the victor, what actions are prohibited, and what constitutes defeat. These rules shall apply to unmarried women as well as to men.

In place of the pancration, we will establish light-armed combat. Competitors will fight with bows, light shields, javelins, and stones thrown by sling or by hand. Laws shall govern these contests, and prizes shall be awarded to those who best adhere to the rules of engagement."
elegant: "Wrestling is replaced by armored combat -- one-on-one up to ten-on-ten -- with expert judges defining the rules of victory and defeat. These apply to women until marriage. The pancration is replaced by light-armed combat with bows, shields, javelins, and slings, with prizes for those who best follow the rules."
summary: The Athenian replaces traditional wrestling with military-oriented armored combat in progressively larger team formats, and substitutes the pancration with light-armed fighting using bows, shields, javelins, and slings. Expert assessors will define the rules of engagement, and these regulations apply to unmarried women as well as men.
distilled: Traditional wrestling and pancration are replaced by armored combat and light-armed fighting respectively, with expert-defined rules applying to both men and unmarried women.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The pancration (pankration) was a brutal ancient Greek combat sport combining elements of boxing and wrestling with almost no rules -- biting and eye-gouging were the only prohibited techniques. Replacing it with light-armed combat reflects the Laws' consistent subordination of athletics to military utility. The team combat format (up to 10v10) has no parallel in actual Greek athletic practice and represents an innovation aimed at training for military unit cohesion. The extension to women until marriage continues the pattern established in the previous passage.

narrative:
"Next in order we shall have to legislate about the horse contests. Now we do not need many horses, for they cannot be of much use in a country like Crete, and hence we naturally do not take great pains about the rearing of them or about horse races. There is no one who keeps a chariot among us, and any rivalry in such matters would be altogether out of place; there would be no sense nor any shadow of sense in instituting contests which are not after the manner of our country.

And therefore we give our prizes for single horses -- for colts who have not yet cast their teeth, and for those who are intermediate, and for the full-grown horses themselves; and thus our equestrian games will accord with the nature of the country. Let them have conflict and rivalry in these matters in accordance with the law, and let the colonels and generals of horse decide together about all courses and about the armed competitors in them. But we have nothing to say to the unarmed either in gymnastic exercises or in these contests.

On the other hand, the Cretan bowman or javelin-man who fights in armour on horseback is useful, and therefore we may as well place a competition of this sort among amusements. Women are not to be forced to compete by laws and ordinances; but if from previous training they have acquired the habit and are strong enough and like to take part, let them do so, girls as well as boys, and no blame to them."
refashioned:
"Next we must legislate regarding equestrian contests. Horses are not particularly useful in a country like Crete, so we naturally do not invest heavily in breeding them or in horse racing. No one among us keeps a chariot, and any competition involving chariots would be completely out of place. It would make no sense whatsoever to establish contests unsuited to our country's terrain and character.

Therefore, our prizes will be for individual horses only -- colts that have not yet shed their first teeth, intermediate horses, and fully grown horses. In this way our equestrian events will suit the nature of our land. The colonels and cavalry generals shall jointly oversee all courses and the armed competitors in them. As always, we have nothing to offer the unarmed, whether in gymnastics or equestrian events.

However, the Cretan mounted archer or javelin-thrower who fights in armor on horseback has genuine military value, so we should include this type of competition among our public exercises. Women shall not be compelled by law to participate in equestrian events; but if their prior training has given them the skill and strength, and they wish to compete, they shall be free to do so -- girls as well as boys -- without any reproach."
elegant:
"Crete has little use for horses, and chariot racing would be absurd here. Prizes go to individual horses only -- colts, intermediates, and fully grown -- suited to our terrain. Cavalry officers oversee all courses. No prizes for the unarmed.

Mounted archery and javelin-throwing in armor have military value and deserve competition. Women are not compelled to compete in equestrian events, but those with skill and desire may do so freely, without blame."
summary: The Athenian tailors equestrian competitions to Crete's geography, eliminating chariot racing in favor of individual horse events overseen by cavalry officers. He includes mounted archery and javelin-throwing as militarily valuable events, and permits but does not require women's participation in equestrian contests.
distilled: Equestrian contests should suit the local terrain, focus on militarily useful mounted combat rather than chariot racing, and permit voluntary female participation.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The adaptation of contests to local geography reflects the Laws' practical realism compared to the Republic's more abstract idealism. Crete's mountainous terrain genuinely made cavalry and chariots less useful than in the plains of Thessaly or the Peloponnese. Chariot racing was the most prestigious event at the Olympic Games, and its exclusion here is a deliberate rejection of aristocratic display culture. The voluntary nature of women's equestrian participation contrasts with the compulsory running in 8.12, perhaps reflecting the greater physical demands and danger of mounted combat. The phrase 'no blame to them' suggests awareness that female equestrian competition would be controversial.

narrative:
"Thus the competition in gymnastic and the mode of learning it have been described; and we have spoken also of the toils of the contest, and of daily exercises under the superintendence of masters. Likewise, what relates to music has been, for the most part, completed.

But as to rhapsodes and the like, and the contests of choruses which are to perform at feasts, all this shall be arranged when the months and days and years have been appointed for Gods and demi-gods, whether every third year, or again every fifth year, or in whatever way or manner the Gods may put into men's minds the distribution and order of them. At the same time, we may expect that the musical contests will be celebrated in their turn by the command of the judges and the director of education and the guardians of the law meeting together for this purpose, and themselves becoming legislators of the times and nature and conditions of the choral contests and of dancing in general.

What they ought severally to be in language and song, and in the admixture of harmony with rhythm and the dance, has been often declared by the original legislator; and his successors ought to follow him, making the games and sacrifices duly to correspond at fitting times, and appointing public festivals. It is not difficult to determine how these and the like matters may have a regular order; nor, again, will the alteration of them do any great good or harm to the state.

There is, however, another matter of great importance and difficulty, concerning which God should legislate, if there were any possibility of obtaining from him an ordinance about it. But seeing that divine aid is not to be had, there appears to be a need of some bold man who specially honours plainness of speech, and will say outright what he thinks best for the city and citizens -- ordaining what is good and convenient for the whole state amid the corruptions of human souls, opposing the mightiest lusts, and having no man his helper but himself standing alone and following reason only."

"What is this, Stranger, that you are saying? For we do not as yet understand your meaning."
refashioned:
"We have now covered gymnastic competition and how it should be taught, including the rigors of contests and daily training under instructors. The subject of music has also been largely addressed.

As for rhapsodic performances and choral competitions at festivals, these details shall be arranged once the calendar of religious observances has been established -- whether festivals are held every three years, every five, or on whatever schedule the gods inspire. The judges, the director of education, and the guardians of the law shall convene to become, in effect, the legislators of choral and dance competitions, determining their timing, character, and conditions.

The original legislator has already established what these performances should be in terms of language, song, harmony, rhythm, and dance. His successors should follow his lead, coordinating games and sacrifices with the appropriate seasons and establishing public festivals. Ordering these matters is not difficult, and minor changes to them will neither greatly benefit nor seriously harm the state.

There is, however, another matter of enormous importance and extreme difficulty -- one that ideally a god would legislate for us, if divine ordinances could be obtained. But since divine intervention is not forthcoming, what is needed is some bold individual who values plain speaking above all else, and who will say openly what he believes is best for the city and its citizens. Such a person would prescribe what is good for the whole state despite the corruptions of human souls, standing in opposition to the most powerful appetites, with no ally but himself and nothing to guide him but reason alone."

"What are you saying, Stranger? We do not yet understand what you mean."
elegant:
"Gymnastic and musical education have been addressed. Festival scheduling -- rhapsodes, choruses, calendar -- can be handled by the judges and guardians of the law following established principles. These details are manageable and their alteration would cause little harm.

But there remains a matter of great importance and extreme difficulty -- one that ideally a god would legislate. Since divine aid is unavailable, what is needed is a bold individual who will speak plainly about what is best for the city, opposing the most powerful human appetites, standing alone with nothing but reason as his guide."

"What do you mean, Stranger? We do not yet understand."
summary: The Athenian summarizes the completion of gymnastic and musical legislation, notes that festival scheduling can be handled by officials following established principles, then dramatically announces a new topic of great importance and difficulty. He says it ideally requires divine legislation, but since that is unavailable, a bold individual must speak plainly about what is best for the state, opposing the strongest human appetites with reason alone. Cleinias is puzzled.
distilled: After completing the relatively manageable topics of athletics and music, the Athenian announces a subject so difficult that it ideally requires divine legislation -- one demanding a bold individual who will oppose the strongest human desires with reason alone.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. This passage is a masterful dramatic transition, building suspense for the discussion of sexual regulation that follows. The image of the lone legislator standing against human appetite with only reason as his guide is one of the most powerful in the Laws and reflects Plato's conception of the philosopher-legislator. The reference to 'the original legislator' likely means the Athenian's earlier pronouncements in Books 2-3 on music and dance. Rhapsodes were professional reciters of epic poetry, especially Homer -- cf. the Ion, Plato's dialogue on rhapsodic art. The frank admission that divine legislation would be preferable to human reflects the Laws' recurring theme of the gap between ideal and achievable governance.

narrative:
"Very likely; I will endeavour to explain myself more clearly. When I came to the subject of education, I beheld young men and maidens holding friendly intercourse with one another. And there naturally arose in my mind a sort of apprehension -- I could not help thinking how one is to deal with a city in which youths and maidens are well nurtured, and have nothing to do, and are not undergoing the excessive and servile toils which extinguish wantonness, and whose only cares during their whole life are sacrifices and festivals and dances.

How, in such a state as this, will they abstain from desires which thrust many a man and woman into perdition; and from which reason, assuming the functions of law, commands them to abstain? The ordinances already made may possibly get the better of most of these desires; the prohibition of excessive wealth is a very considerable gain in the direction of temperance, and the whole education of our youth imposes a law of moderation on them; moreover, the eye of the rulers is required always to watch over the young, and never to lose sight of them; and these provisions do, as far as human means can effect anything, exercise a regulating influence upon the desires in general.

But how can we take precautions against the unnatural loves of either sex, from which innumerable evils have come upon individuals and cities? How shall we devise a remedy and way of escape out of so great a danger? Truly, Cleinias, here is a difficulty. In many ways Crete and Lacedaemon furnish a great help to those who make peculiar laws; but in the matter of love, as we are alone, I must confess that they are quite against us."
refashioned:
"That is understandable. Let me try to be clearer. When I turned to the subject of education, I observed young men and women freely socializing with one another, and a certain apprehension naturally arose in my mind. How does one manage a city where the youth are well-fed, well-educated, and idle -- where they do not endure the harsh, servile labor that typically suppresses excessive desire, and where their only concerns throughout life are sacrifices, festivals, and dances?

In such a state, how will they restrain themselves from the desires that have driven so many men and women to ruin -- desires that reason, acting in the role of law, commands them to resist? The laws we have already established may overcome most of these temptations. The prohibition of excessive wealth contributes substantially to temperance. Our entire educational system instills moderation. The rulers are required to keep constant watch over the young, never letting them out of sight. These measures, so far as human effort can achieve anything, do regulate desire in general.

But how do we guard against unnatural sexual desires in both sexes -- desires that have brought countless evils upon individuals and cities alike? How do we find a remedy for so great a danger? Here, Cleinias, is a genuine difficulty. In many respects Crete and Sparta provide invaluable models for unusual legislation. But on the matter of love, I must confess, their customs work against us rather than for us."
elegant: "When I considered our young citizens -- well-nurtured, idle, free from harsh labor, occupied only with festivals and dances -- I grew apprehensive. How will they resist the desires that ruin so many? Our existing laws against wealth, our education in moderation, and the rulers' constant oversight can regulate most desires. But against unnatural loves, which have brought innumerable evils upon individuals and cities, we lack a remedy. Crete and Sparta, helpful in so many legislative matters, are on this point against us."
summary: The Athenian explains his earlier cryptic warning: in a prosperous city where young citizens are idle and well-fed, sexual desire becomes a serious danger. While existing laws on wealth, education, and surveillance can regulate most appetites, they cannot adequately address same-sex desire, which has caused enormous harm. He admits that Cretan and Spartan customs, usually helpful legislative models, actually exacerbate this particular problem.
distilled: A prosperous city of idle, well-nurtured youth faces an acute danger from sexual desire that existing laws cannot fully address, and Cretan and Spartan customs offer no help on this point.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage begins Plato's extended and controversial treatment of sexual regulation (8.16-8.28). The reference to Crete and Sparta being 'against us' alludes to the institutionalized pederastic relationships in both societies -- cf. Xenophon, Constitution of the Lacedaemonians 2.12-14 and Strabo 10.4.21 on Cretan practices. The concern about idle, prosperous youth echoes Republic 421d-422a on the dangers of wealth and leisure. The idea that harsh labor 'extinguishes wantonness' connects to the Laws' broader ambivalence about the relationship between prosperity and virtue. The Athenian's apprehension about well-fed youth socializing freely reflects Plato's consistent concern with regulating eros -- cf. Symposium, Phaedrus, and Republic 403a-c.

narrative:
"For if any one following nature should lay down the law which existed before the days of Laius, and denounce these lusts as contrary to nature, adducing the animals as a proof that such unions were monstrous, he might prove his point, but he would be wholly at variance with the custom of your states.

Further, they are repugnant to a principle which we say that a legislator should always observe; for we are always enquiring which of our enactments tends to virtue and which not. And suppose we grant that these loves are accounted by law to be honourable, or at least not disgraceful, in what degree will they contribute to virtue? Will such passions implant in the soul of him who is seduced the habit of courage, or in the soul of the seducer the principle of temperance? Who will ever believe this? -- or rather, who will not blame the effeminacy of him who yields to pleasures and is unable to hold out against them? Will not all men censure as womanly him who imitates the woman?

And who would ever think of establishing such a practice by law? Certainly no one who had in his mind the image of true law. How can we prove that what I am saying is true? He who would rightly consider these matters must see the nature of friendship and desire, and of these so-called loves, for they are of two kinds, and out of the two arises a third kind, having the same name; and this similarity of name causes all the difficulty and obscurity."

"How is that?"

"Dear is the like in virtue to the like, and the equal to the equal; dear also, though unlike, is he who has abundance to him who is in want. And when either of these friendships becomes excessive, we term the excess love."

"Very true."
refashioned:
"If someone, following nature, were to revive the law that existed before the days of Laius and condemn these desires as contrary to nature -- pointing to animal behavior as proof that such unions are unnatural -- he might well prove his case, but he would find himself completely at odds with the customs of your states.

Moreover, these customs conflict with the principle that a legislator should always keep in view: namely, which laws promote virtue and which do not. Even if we grant that these relationships are considered honorable, or at least not shameful, by law -- to what extent do they cultivate virtue? Do such passions instill courage in the soul of the pursued, or temperance in the soul of the pursuer? No one would believe this. On the contrary, everyone would condemn the weakness of someone who surrenders to pleasure and cannot resist it. Everyone would censure as effeminate the one who takes on the passive role.

No one with a true understanding of law would think of sanctioning such practices. But how can we demonstrate this? Anyone who wants to think clearly about these matters must first understand the nature of friendship and desire. There are two kinds of what we call 'love,' and from these two a third kind arises, which shares the same name. This ambiguity of terminology is the source of all the confusion."

"How so?"

"The virtuous love the virtuous, the like is dear to the like, and equals are drawn to equals. But there is also an attraction between opposites -- the one who has abundance is drawn to the one in need. When either of these forms of friendship grows excessive, we call the excess 'love.'"

"Very true."
elegant:
"One could invoke nature and animal behavior to condemn these desires, but that would contradict your states' customs. The real test is virtue: do such passions produce courage in the beloved or temperance in the lover? Clearly not. No true legislator would sanction them.

The confusion arises from language. There are two kinds of friendship: between equals in virtue, and between those who have and those who lack. When either grows excessive, we call it 'love' -- and this shared name causes all the difficulty."
summary: The Athenian argues against same-sex relationships on both natural and ethical grounds, contending that they promote neither courage nor temperance. He then introduces a crucial distinction: there are two kinds of friendship (between equals and between unequals), and when either becomes excessive it is called love. The shared term causes confusion, and sorting out these types is essential to sound legislation.
distilled: Same-sex relations fail the legislator's virtue test because they produce neither courage nor temperance, and the confusion surrounding 'love' stems from collapsing two fundamentally different types of friendship under one name.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. Laius, king of Thebes and father of Oedipus, was traditionally considered the originator of pederasty in Greek mythology. The appeal to animal behavior as evidence of natural law is an early instance of the natural law argument. The distinction between two kinds of love (eros of similarity vs. eros of complementarity) develops ideas from the Symposium (especially Pausanias's speech at 180c-185c on heavenly vs. common love) and the Phaedrus (249d-257b on philosophical eros). The critique that the passive partner's 'effeminacy' undermines courage reflects widespread Greek attitudes about gender and sexuality, as documented in Dover's Greek Homosexuality. This passage represents Plato's most explicitly critical treatment of pederasty, contrasting with the more nuanced treatments in the Symposium and Phaedrus.

narrative:
"The friendship which arises from contraries is horrible and coarse, and has often no tie of communion; but that which arises from likeness is gentle, and has a tie of communion which lasts through life.

As to the mixed sort which is made up of them both, there is, first of all, a difficulty in determining what he who is possessed by this third love desires; moreover, he is drawn different ways, and is in doubt between the two principles; the one exhorting him to enjoy the beauty of youth, and the other forbidding him. For the one is a lover of the body, and hungers after beauty, like ripe fruit, and would fain satisfy himself without any regard to the character of the beloved; the other holds the desire of the body to be a secondary matter, and looking rather than loving and with his soul desiring the soul of the other in a becoming manner, regards the satisfaction of the bodily love as wantonness; he reverences and respects temperance and courage and magnanimity and wisdom, and wishes to live chastely with the chaste object of his affection.

Now the sort of love which is made up of the other two is that which we have described as the third. Seeing then that there are these three sorts of love, ought the law to prohibit and forbid them all to exist among us? Is it not rather clear that we should wish to have in the state the love which is of virtue and which desires the beloved youth to be the best possible; and the other two, if possible, we should hinder? What do you say, friend Megillus?"
refashioned:
"The friendship born from opposites -- where one has what the other lacks -- is crude and coarse, and often has no basis for genuine communion. But the friendship born from likeness is gentle, and creates a bond of communion that lasts a lifetime.

The third kind, which combines elements of both, is the most perplexing. It is difficult to determine what the person possessed by this mixed love truly wants. He is pulled in two directions, torn between two competing impulses: one urges him to enjoy the physical beauty of youth, while the other forbids it. The first is a lover of the body who hungers after beauty as one craves ripe fruit, seeking satisfaction without any regard for the beloved's character. The second considers bodily desire a secondary matter; he gazes rather than grasps, his soul reaching toward the other's soul in a fitting manner. He views bodily gratification as wantonness, reverences temperance, courage, magnanimity, and wisdom, and wishes to live purely alongside the pure object of his devotion.

The love composed of both these impulses is what we have called the third kind. Given that there are these three forms of love, should the law ban all of them? Surely not. It is clear that we should encourage the love rooted in virtue -- the love that desires the beloved to become the best possible person -- while hindering the other two forms as far as we can. What do you say, friend Megillus?"
elegant:
"Love born from opposites is coarse and lacks communion. Love born from likeness is gentle and lifelong. The third kind -- a mixture -- is the most confusing, pulling the lover between bodily desire and spiritual aspiration. One impulse hungers after beauty like ripe fruit, heedless of character; the other reverences virtue and seeks soul-to-soul communion.

The law should encourage the love that desires the beloved's moral perfection, and hinder the other two."
summary: The Athenian distinguishes three forms of love: coarse desire born from opposites, gentle communion born from similarity, and a conflicted mixture of both. In the mixed form, one impulse seeks physical gratification regardless of character, while the other aspires to soul-to-soul connection grounded in virtue. He argues that the law should promote only the virtuous form that seeks the beloved's moral improvement, while discouraging the other two.
distilled: Of the three forms of love -- physical, spiritual, and mixed -- the law should promote only the spiritual love that seeks the beloved's moral perfection and discourage the rest.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This taxonomy of love builds directly on the Symposium's speeches, especially Pausanias's distinction between 'heavenly' (Ouranian) and 'common' (Pandemian) Aphrodite (Symposium 180d-181d), and Diotima's ladder of love (Symposium 210a-212a). The image of beauty as 'ripe fruit' is a memorable metaphor for purely physical desire. The ideal of soul-to-soul love echoes the Phaedrus 252d-256e on the philosopher-lover who restrains physical desire. The turn to Megillus (a Spartan) rather than Cleinias (a Cretan) is significant, as Sparta had formalized pederastic relationships within its educational system. The conclusion that law should promote virtue-oriented love while suppressing other forms represents Plato's most legislative treatment of eros.

narrative:
"I think, Stranger, that you are perfectly right in what you have been now saying."

"I knew well, my friend, that I should obtain your assent, which I accept, and therefore have no need to analyse your custom any further. Cleinias shall be prevailed upon to give me his assent at some other time. Enough of this; and now let us proceed to the laws."

"Very good."

"Upon reflection I see a way of imposing the law, which, in one respect, is easy, but, in another, is of the utmost difficulty."

"What do you mean?"

"We are all aware that most men, in spite of their lawless natures, are very strictly and precisely restrained from intercourse with the fair, and this is not at all against their will, but entirely with their will."

"When do you mean?"

"When any one has a brother or sister who is fair; and about a son or daughter the same unwritten law holds, and is a most perfect safeguard, so that no open or secret connection ever takes place between them. Nor does the thought of such a thing ever enter at all into the minds of most of them."

"Very true."

"Does not a little word extinguish all pleasures of that sort?"

"What word?"
refashioned:
"I think you are entirely right in what you have just said, Stranger."

"I knew I would have your agreement, my friend, and I accept it gratefully. There is no need for me to examine your customs further on this point. I will secure Cleinias's agreement at another time. But enough of this -- let us move on to the actual laws."

"Very good."

"On reflection, I can see a way of establishing this law that is, in one respect, easy, but in another, extremely difficult."

"What do you mean?"

"We all know that most people, despite their generally lawless natures, are very strictly and precisely restrained from sexual relations with beautiful family members -- and this restraint is not against their will but entirely in accordance with it."

"What situation are you thinking of?"

"When someone has a beautiful brother or sister. The same unwritten law applies to one's son or daughter, and it serves as a perfectly effective safeguard. No open or secret liaison ever occurs between them. The very thought never even enters most people's minds."

"That is perfectly true."

"And does not a single word extinguish all such desire?"

"What word?"
elegant:
Megillus agrees. The Athenian accepts and moves to the practical question of legislation, which is easy in one respect but extremely difficult in another.

He observes that the incest taboo provides a model: most people, however lawless, are perfectly restrained from sexual desire toward family members -- not by force but by willing conviction. The thought never even enters their minds.

"A single word extinguishes all such desire."

"What word?"
summary: After securing Megillus's agreement, the Athenian proposes a legislative strategy by pointing to the incest taboo as a model. Despite their generally lawless natures, most people willingly and completely restrain themselves from sexual interest in family members, and the very thought never occurs to them. He asks what single word accomplishes this remarkable suppression of desire.
distilled: The incest taboo proves that a single deeply held social conviction can completely extinguish even the most powerful sexual desires, providing a model for broader sexual legislation.
notes: Speakers: Megillus and Athenian Stranger. The incest taboo argument is one of Plato's most sociologically astute observations, anticipating modern anthropological analysis of taboo formation. The insight that social conditioning can make certain desires not merely forbidden but literally unthinkable is remarkably sophisticated. The diplomatic handling of Megillus (accepting his assent without pressing on Spartan customs) and the deferral of Cleinias's agreement show the Athenian's political sensitivity. The principle that willing restraint through conviction is superior to forced compliance through punishment is central to the Laws' legislative philosophy -- cf. the distinction between the law's 'prelude' (persuasion) and its 'song' (compulsion) at Laws 722d-723d.

narrative: "The declaration that they are unholy, hated of God, and most infamous; and is not the reason of this that no one has ever said the opposite, but every one from his earliest childhood has heard men speaking in the same manner about them always and everywhere, whether in comedy or in the graver language of tragedy?
