When the poet introduces on the stage a Thyestes or an Oedipus, or a Macareus having secret intercourse with his sister, he represents him, when found out, ready to kill himself as the penalty of his sin."
refashioned: "The word is this: that these acts are unholy, hated by the gods, and utterly infamous. And the reason this word has such power is that no one has ever contradicted it. From earliest childhood, every person hears the same condemnation expressed always and everywhere -- in comedy and in the solemn language of tragedy alike. Whenever a poet brings a Thyestes or an Oedipus onto the stage, or a Macareus who has had secret relations with his sister, the character is always shown, once discovered, as ready to kill himself as the penalty for his transgression."
elegant: "The word: that these acts are unholy, hated by the gods, and utterly infamous. Its power comes from unanimity -- no one has ever contradicted it. From childhood, everyone hears the same condemnation everywhere, in comedy and tragedy alike. Thyestes, Oedipus, Macareus -- every poet shows them ready to die once their incest is discovered."
summary: The Athenian reveals that the single word suppressing incestuous desire is the unanimous declaration that such acts are unholy, god-hated, and infamous. Its power derives from the fact that no one has ever contradicted it: from earliest childhood, everyone encounters the same condemnation in all cultural forms, from comedy to tragedy, where characters like Thyestes and Oedipus are shown choosing death upon discovery.
distilled: The incest taboo's power lies in the unanimity of cultural condemnation -- from childhood, no one ever hears a contrary voice, and every artistic portrayal reinforces the same message.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. Thyestes committed incest with his daughter Pelopia; Oedipus unknowingly married his mother Jocasta; Macareus (son of Aeolus) had an affair with his sister Canace -- all standard mythological exempla of incest. The argument that cultural unanimity creates unthinkable taboos is a powerful insight about the relationship between social consensus and moral psychology. This passage implicitly argues for state control of artistic expression, since the taboo depends on poets never presenting a sympathetic portrayal of incest -- connecting to Republic 377-403 on the censorship of poetry. The theory of moral formation through consistent cultural messaging anticipates modern social learning theory.

narrative:
"You are very right in saying that tradition, if no breath of opposition ever assails it, has a marvellous power."

"Am I not also right in saying that the legislator who wants to master any of the passions which master man may easily know how to subdue them? He will consecrate the tradition of their evil character among all, slaves and freemen, women and children, throughout the city: -- that will be the surest foundation of the law which he can make."

"Yes; but will he ever succeed in making all mankind use the same language about them?"
refashioned:
"You are quite right that tradition, so long as it is never challenged, possesses an extraordinary power."

"And am I not also right to say that a legislator who wishes to overcome any of the passions that dominate human beings can easily discover the method? He must establish a universal tradition that these behaviors are evil -- among all citizens, slaves and free, women and children, throughout the entire city. That will be the most secure foundation any law can have."

"Yes, but will he ever actually succeed in getting all of humanity to speak with one voice on these matters?"
elegant:
Megillus agrees that unopposed tradition has extraordinary power. The Athenian adds that a legislator can subdue any passion by consecrating the tradition of its evil throughout all classes of society -- that is the surest legal foundation.

Megillus raises the critical objection: can universal consensus actually be achieved?
summary: Megillus affirms the power of unopposed tradition, and the Athenian argues that any passion can be subdued if the legislator universally consecrates its condemnation among all classes. Megillus raises the practical objection of whether truly universal consensus is achievable.
distilled: A legislator can subdue any passion by consecrating universal condemnation of it, but achieving truly unanimous consensus remains the practical challenge.
notes: Speakers: Megillus and Athenian Stranger. Megillus's objection is genuinely penetrating -- it identifies the gap between the theoretical power of unanimous tradition and the practical difficulty of achieving it. This echoes a tension running throughout the Laws between ideal legislation and practical implementation. The idea that the legislator must shape opinion among 'slaves and freemen, women and children' reflects the Laws' comprehensive vision of social engineering through cultural formation -- cf. Laws 659d-660a on the power of consistent moral messaging. The phrase 'consecrate the tradition' (kathieroun) carries religious overtones, suggesting that moral norms must have quasi-sacred status to be effective.

narrative:
"A good objection; but was I not just now saying that I had a way to make men use natural love and abstain from unnatural, not intentionally destroying the seeds of human increase, or sowing them in stony places, in which they will take no root; and that I would command them to abstain too from any female field of increase in which that which is sown is not likely to grow?

Now if a law to this effect could only be made perpetual, and gain an authority such as already prevents intercourse of parents and children -- such a law, extending to other sensual desires, and conquering them, would be the source of ten thousand blessings. For, in the first place, moderation is the appointment of nature, and deters men from all frenzy and madness of love, and from all adulteries and immoderate use of meats and drinks, and makes them good friends to their own wives. And innumerable other benefits would result if such a law could only be enforced.

I can imagine some lusty youth who is standing by, and who, on hearing this enactment, declares in scurrilous terms that we are making foolish and impossible laws, and fills the world with his outcry. And therefore I said that I knew a way of enacting and perpetuating such a law, which was very easy in one respect, but in another most difficult.

There is no difficulty in seeing that such a law is possible, and in what way; for, as I was saying, the ordinance once consecrated would master the soul of every man, and terrify him into obedience. But matters have now come to such a pass that even then the desired result seems as if it could not be attained, just as the continuance of an entire state in the practice of common meals is also deemed impossible. And although this latter is partly disproven by the fact of their existence among you, still even in your cities the common meals of women would be regarded as unnatural and impossible. I was thinking of the rebelliousness of the human heart when I said that the permanent establishment of these things is very difficult."
refashioned:
"A fair objection. But did I not just say that I had a way to direct people toward natural love and away from unnatural -- to prevent them from deliberately destroying the seeds of human reproduction, or sowing them where they will never take root? I would also command them to refrain from any sexual union unlikely to produce offspring.

If a law to this effect could be made permanent and gain the same authority that already prevents sexual relations between parents and children, then such a law -- extended to govern other sensual desires and conquering them -- would be the source of countless blessings. First, moderation is nature's own design, and it deters people from the frenzy and madness of desire, from adultery, and from excessive indulgence in food and drink. It makes them faithful companions to their own spouses. Innumerable other benefits would follow if this law could be enforced.

I can already picture some lusty young man standing nearby, hearing this proposal, and loudly declaring in crude language that we are making stupid and impossible laws. His outrage would echo everywhere. This is exactly why I said I knew a method for establishing such a law that was very easy in one respect but enormously difficult in another.

It is not hard to see that such a law is possible in principle: once consecrated, it would master every person's soul and compel obedience through a kind of moral terror. But things have deteriorated to the point where even this approach seems unable to achieve the desired result -- just as maintaining universal common meals throughout an entire state is considered impossible. Now, this latter claim is partly disproven by the fact that common meals actually exist among you. Yet even in your cities, the idea of common meals for women would be dismissed as unnatural and impossible. When I said that permanently establishing these things is very difficult, I was thinking of the sheer rebelliousness of the human heart."
elegant:
"I proposed directing people toward natural reproduction and away from barren or unnatural unions. If this law could gain the same authority as the incest taboo, it would bring countless blessings -- moderation, fidelity, temperance in all appetites.

Some lusty youth will surely mock this as foolish and impossible. The principle is easy enough: consecrated tradition can master any soul. But in practice, the rebelliousness of the human heart makes permanent establishment extremely difficult -- just as common meals, though they exist among you, are considered impossible for women even in your own cities."
summary: The Athenian argues that a law directing sexuality toward natural reproduction, if given the same sacrosanct authority as the incest taboo, would bring immense benefits including moderation, fidelity, and temperance. He acknowledges that some will mock these laws as impossible, and concedes that while the principle of consecrated tradition is sound, the rebelliousness of the human heart makes permanent implementation extremely difficult -- as illustrated by the fact that even existing common-meal customs cannot be extended to women.
distilled: A law channeling sexuality toward natural reproduction could bring vast benefits if given taboo-level authority, but the rebelliousness of human nature makes permanent enforcement extremely difficult.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The agricultural metaphors ('sowing in stony places,' 'female field of increase') for sexual regulation are characteristic of ancient Greek thinking about reproduction. The imagined 'lusty youth' who mocks the legislation is a vivid rhetorical device showing Plato's awareness of resistance to sexual regulation. The appeal to common meals (syssitia) in Crete and Sparta as partial proof of possibility is a recurring strategy in the Laws -- using existing practices to demonstrate feasibility. The admission about the 'rebelliousness of the human heart' is one of the Laws' most psychologically honest moments, reflecting Plato's mature recognition that ideal legislation must contend with intractable human nature -- contrast with the Republic's more optimistic expectations about philosophical education transforming desire.

narrative:
"Very true."

"Shall I try and find some sort of persuasive argument which will prove to you that such enactments are possible, and not beyond human nature?"

"By all means."

"Is a man more likely to abstain from the pleasures of love and to do what he is bidden about them, when his body is in a good condition, or when he is in an ill condition, and out of training?"

"He will be far more temperate when he is in training."
refashioned:
"Very true."

"Then shall I attempt to find a persuasive argument proving that such laws are possible and do not exceed human capability?"

"Please do."

"Tell me: is a person more likely to resist sexual temptation and obey the laws governing it when his body is in excellent condition and well-trained, or when he is in poor health and out of shape?"

"He will be far more temperate when he is in good training."
elegant:
The Athenian offers to prove these laws are humanly possible. He asks whether a well-trained body or an untrained one is better at resisting sexual temptation.

The answer: a trained body is far more temperate.
summary: The Athenian proposes a persuasive argument for the feasibility of sexual regulation. He establishes the premise that physical fitness and training produce greater sexual temperance than physical neglect, which Cleinias readily confirms.
distilled: Physical fitness and disciplined training make a person more capable of sexual self-control, not less.
notes: Speakers: Megillus, Athenian Stranger, and Cleinias. The counterintuitive claim that physical fitness promotes temperance rather than increasing desire challenges the common assumption that vigorous bodies are more sexually driven. This connects to the Laws' broader integration of physical and moral education. The argument anticipates the athletic examples in the next passage (8.24). The Socratic method of building an argument through agreed premises is characteristic of Plato's dialogues, though the Laws uses it more sparingly than the earlier works.

narrative: "And have we not heard of Iccus of Tarentum, who, with a view to the Olympic and other contests, in his zeal for his art, and also because he was of a manly and temperate disposition, never had any connection with a woman or a youth during the whole time of his training? And the same is said of Crison and Astylus and Diopompus and many others; and yet, Cleinias, they were far worse educated in their minds than your and my citizens, and in their bodies far more lusty."
refashioned: "Have we not heard of Iccus of Tarentum, who, driven by his dedication to the Olympic and other competitions -- and also by his naturally courageous and temperate character -- abstained from all sexual contact, with women or youths alike, throughout his entire period of training? The same is reported of Crison, Astylus, Diopompus, and many others. And yet, Cleinias, these athletes were far less educated in mind than the citizens you and I are designing, and far more physically vigorous and sexually robust."
elegant: "Iccus of Tarentum abstained from all sexual contact during his Olympic training, as did Crison, Astylus, Diopompus, and many others. Yet these athletes were less educated than our citizens and far more physically vigorous. If they could manage it, surely our better-educated citizens can too."
summary: The Athenian cites famous Greek athletes -- Iccus of Tarentum, Crison, Astylus, and Diopompus -- who practiced complete sexual abstinence during their training periods despite being less intellectually educated and more physically vigorous than the citizens of the proposed state. If these athletes could exercise such restraint for mere athletic victory, the proposed citizens should be capable of the same for a far nobler purpose.
distilled: If less-educated, more physically vigorous athletes like Iccus of Tarentum could practice complete sexual abstinence for athletic victory, our better-educated citizens can certainly do the same for a higher purpose.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. Iccus of Tarentum was a famous 5th-century BCE Olympic victor and trainer, also mentioned in Protagoras 316d. Astylus of Croton was a celebrated Olympic sprinter (488-480 BCE). Crison and Diopompus are less well-documented athletes. The argument from athletic abstinence was a common rhetorical topos in ancient Greek discourse on self-control. The logic -- that if those with worse education and stronger physical appetites could manage abstinence, then better-educated citizens can too -- is an a fortiori argument. This passage connects physical discipline to moral virtue, a theme central to Greek paideia and developed extensively in the Republic's treatment of gymnastic education (403c-412a).

narrative:
"No doubt this fact has been often affirmed positively by the ancients of these athletes."

"And had they courage to abstain from what is ordinarily deemed a pleasure for the sake of a victory in wrestling, running, and the like; and shall our young men be incapable of a similar endurance for the sake of a much nobler victory, which is the noblest of all, as from their youth upwards we will tell them, charming them, as we hope, into the belief of this by tales and sayings and songs?"

"Of what victory are you speaking?"

"Of the victory over pleasure, which if they win, they will live happily; or if they are conquered, the reverse of happily. And, further, may we not suppose that the fear of impiety will enable them to master that which other inferior people have mastered?"

"I dare say."
refashioned:
"Indeed, these accounts of the athletes' abstinence have been widely confirmed by tradition."

"So these athletes had the courage to forgo what most people consider pleasure for the sake of victory in wrestling, running, and similar contests. Shall our young citizens then be incapable of similar endurance for the sake of a far nobler victory -- the noblest of all? From their youth onward, we will teach them this through the charm of stories, proverbs, and songs, instilling in them the belief that this is the greatest victory of all."

"What victory do you mean?"

"The victory over pleasure. Those who win it will live happily; those who are defeated by pleasure will live miserably. Furthermore, can we not expect that the fear of impiety will give them the strength to master what others, far less disciplined than they, have already mastered?"

"I would think so."
elegant:
"If athletes could forgo pleasure for mere wrestling victories, our citizens can surely endure the same for the noblest victory of all -- victory over pleasure itself. Those who win it live happily; those who lose, miserably. And the fear of impiety should enable them to master what lesser people have already mastered."

"I would think so."
summary: The Athenian argues that if ancient athletes could abstain from pleasure for the sake of athletic victory, the proposed citizens should be capable of even greater endurance for the far nobler victory over pleasure itself. He proposes using stories, proverbs, and songs to instill this belief from youth, and adds that the fear of impiety should provide additional motivation.
distilled: If athletes abstained from pleasure for mere sporting victories, citizens can surely do the same for the noblest victory of all -- mastery over pleasure -- reinforced by the fear of impiety.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. The concept of 'victory over pleasure' as the noblest contest echoes Laws 1.626e, where the Athenian argues that self-mastery is the most important form of victory. The strategy of using 'tales and sayings and songs' to charm youth into belief connects to the Laws' extensive treatment of educational music and poetry (Books 2-3, 7). The 'fear of impiety' as a motivating force reflects the Laws' departure from the Republic's more philosophical approach to motivation -- here, religious feeling supplements rational persuasion. The phrase 'the reverse of happily' (to enantion) is a characteristic Platonic understatement. Cleinias's guarded response ('I dare say') suggests he is not entirely convinced.

narrative:
"And since we have reached this point in our legislation, and have fallen into a difficulty by reason of the vices of mankind, I affirm that our ordinance should simply run in the following terms: Our citizens ought not to fall below the nature of birds and beasts in general, who are born in great multitudes, and yet remain until the age for procreation virgin and unmarried, but when they have reached the proper time of life are coupled, male and female, and lovingly pair together, and live the rest of their lives in holiness and innocence, abiding firmly in their original compact: -- surely, we will say to them, you should be better than the animals.

But if they are corrupted by the other Hellenes and the common practice of barbarians, and they see with their eyes and hear with their ears of the so-called free love everywhere prevailing among them, and they themselves are not able to get the better of the temptation, the guardians of the law, exercising the functions of lawgivers, shall devise a second law against them."

"And what law would you advise them to pass if this one failed?"

"Clearly, Cleinias, the one which would naturally follow."

"What is that?"
refashioned:
"Since we have reached this point in our legislation and find ourselves confronting the difficulty posed by human vice, I propose that our ordinance should state simply this: our citizens ought not to sink below the standard set by birds and beasts. These creatures are born in great numbers, yet remain virgin and unpaired until the age of procreation. When the proper time arrives, they couple -- male with female -- pair together lovingly, and live out the rest of their lives in faithfulness and innocence, holding firmly to their original bond. Surely, we will say to our citizens, you ought to be better than the animals.

But if our citizens are corrupted by the influence of other Greeks and the common practices of non-Greeks -- if they witness the so-called sexual freedom prevailing everywhere and cannot resist the temptation themselves -- then the guardians of the law, acting as legislators, shall devise a second, stricter law to govern them."

"What law would you recommend if this first one fails?"

"The one that naturally follows, Cleinias."

"Which is?"
elegant:
"Our ordinance should state: citizens must not fall below the standard of animals, who remain virgin until the proper age, then pair faithfully for life. Surely humans should be better than beasts.

But if they are corrupted by the sexual permissiveness prevailing among other Greeks and barbarians, the guardians of the law shall devise a stricter second law."

"What law if this one fails?"

"The one that naturally follows."
summary: The Athenian proposes a primary law invoking the example of animals, who remain chaste until maturity and then pair faithfully for life, arguing that citizens should at minimum match this natural standard. If citizens fail this standard due to corruption from the sexual permissiveness of surrounding cultures, the guardians of the law shall impose a stricter backup law.
distilled: Citizens should at least match the faithfulness of animals who pair for life, but if corrupted by surrounding cultures, a stricter backup law must be devised.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The appeal to animal behavior as a moral standard inverts the usual Greek hierarchy of human over animal -- here, animals set the higher standard of sexual fidelity. This connects to 8.17's use of animal behavior in the argument from nature. The idealized picture of animal monogamy is zoologically inaccurate but rhetorically effective. The two-tier legislative strategy -- an ideal law plus a fallback -- is characteristic of the Laws' practical realism. The reference to 'other Hellenes and barbarians' acknowledges the cultural context of sexual permissiveness that the proposed city must resist. The concept of 'guardians of the law devising a second law' shows the Laws' flexible legislative framework, where officials can adapt laws to changing circumstances.

narrative:
"Our citizens should not allow pleasures to strengthen with indulgence, but should by toil divert the aliment and exuberance of them into other parts of the body; and this will happen if no immodesty be allowed in the practice of love. Then they will be ashamed of frequent intercourse, and they will find pleasure, if seldom enjoyed, to be a less imperious mistress.

They should not be found out doing anything of the sort. Concealment shall be honourable, and sanctioned by custom and made law by unwritten prescription; on the other hand, to be detected shall be esteemed dishonourable, but not to abstain wholly. In this way there will be a second legal standard of honourable and dishonourable, involving a second notion of right.

Three principles will comprehend all those corrupt natures whom we call inferior to themselves, and who form but one class, and will compel them not to transgress."

"What are they?"
refashioned:
"Citizens must not let their pleasures grow stronger through indulgence. Instead, through physical toil, they should redirect the body's excess energy into other channels. This will happen naturally if no shamelessness is permitted in sexual matters. People will then feel ashamed of frequent sexual activity, and they will discover that pleasure, when rarely enjoyed, becomes a far less tyrannical master.

They should not be caught engaging in sexual misconduct. Discretion shall be considered honorable and sanctioned by custom as an unwritten law. Being discovered, on the other hand, shall be considered shameful -- though total abstinence will not be required. In this way, a second standard of honor and dishonor will emerge, embodying a secondary conception of moral right.

Three principles will govern all those people of corrupt nature -- those whom we describe as inferior to themselves, who together form a single class -- and these principles will compel them to stay within bounds."

"What are they?"
elegant:
"Citizens must redirect excess physical energy through toil rather than feeding pleasures through indulgence. If shamelessness in sexual matters is forbidden, people will feel ashamed of excess and find that rare pleasure is a less tyrannical master.

Discretion shall be honorable; being caught, shameful -- though total abstinence is not required. This creates a secondary standard of honor.

Three principles will restrain those of corrupt nature."

"What are they?"
summary: The Athenian outlines a fallback sexual regulation for those who cannot achieve the ideal: physical labor should redirect excess energy, modesty should make frequent sexual activity shameful, and discretion should be honored while detection brings disgrace (though total abstinence is not required). This creates a secondary moral standard. Three governing principles will restrain those of weaker nature.
distilled: For those who cannot achieve complete temperance, the law creates a secondary standard: redirect energy through labor, honor discretion, and shame exposure, with three principles restraining corrupt natures.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The idea of redirecting sexual energy through physical labor anticipates the Freudian concept of sublimation by over two millennia. The distinction between ideal and secondary standards of behavior is a key feature of the Laws' practical approach to legislation -- cf. Laws 739a-e on 'second-best' constitutions. The notion that concealment is honorable while detection is shameful creates an unusual moral framework where the social fact of discovery, rather than the act itself, determines dishonor. The phrase 'inferior to themselves' (hettous hautou) -- people who cannot control their own desires -- is a recurring concept in Plato; cf. Republic 430e-431b on self-mastery. The three principles (piety, honor, and beauty of soul) will be revealed in the next passage.

narrative:
"The principle of piety, the love of honour, and the desire of beauty, not in the body but in the soul. These are, perhaps, romantic aspirations; but they are the noblest of aspirations, if they could only be realized in all states, and, God willing, in the matter of love we may be able to enforce one of two things -- either that no one shall venture to touch any person of the freeborn or noble class except his wedded wife, or sow the unconsecrated and bastard seed among harlots, or in barren and unnatural lusts; or at least we may abolish altogether the connection of men with men; and as to women, if any man has to do with any but those who come into his house duly married by sacred rites, whether they be bought or acquired in any other way, and he offends publicly in the face of all mankind, we shall be right in enacting that he be deprived of civic honours and privileges, and be deemed to be, as he truly is, a stranger.

Let this law, then, whether it is one, or ought rather to be called two, be laid down respecting love in general, and the intercourse of the sexes which arises out of the desires, whether rightly or wrongly indulged."

"I, for my part, Stranger, would gladly receive this law. Cleinias shall speak for himself, and tell you what is his opinion."

"I will, Megillus, when an opportunity offers; at present, I think that we had better allow the Stranger to proceed with his laws."

"Very good."
refashioned:
"The three principles are: piety, the love of honor, and the desire for beauty -- not physical beauty, but beauty of the soul. These may seem like romantic ideals, but they are the noblest of aspirations. If they could be realized in every state, then God willing, in the matter of sexual conduct we might enforce one of two standards.

The first and higher standard: that no one shall touch any freeborn or noble person other than their lawfully wedded spouse, nor sow illegitimate seed among prostitutes, nor engage in barren and unnatural sexual acts.

The second and minimum standard: that at the very least, sexual relations between men shall be abolished entirely. As for relations with women: any man who has relations with any woman other than his lawfully and ceremonially married wife -- whether she was acquired by purchase or any other means -- and who commits this offense publicly and in full view, shall rightly be deprived of all civic honors and privileges, and shall be regarded as what he truly is: a stranger to the city.

Let this law -- whether we should call it one law or two -- be established to govern love in general and all sexual relations arising from desire, whether rightly or wrongly pursued."

"For my part, Stranger, I would gladly accept this law. Cleinias can speak for himself and share his own opinion."

"I will, Megillus, when the opportunity arises. For now, I think we should let the Stranger continue with his legislation."

"Very good."
elegant:
"The three principles: piety, love of honor, and desire for beauty of soul. Noble aspirations, perhaps romantic, but if realized, we could enforce one of two standards -- either that no one touches any freeborn person except their spouse, avoiding both prostitution and unnatural unions; or at minimum, that same-sex relations are abolished and public infidelity is punished by loss of civic status.

Let this law govern all sexual conduct."

Megillus accepts. Cleinias defers. They proceed.
summary: The Athenian reveals the three restraining principles -- piety, love of honor, and desire for spiritual beauty -- and proposes a two-tiered sexual law. The ideal prohibits all extramarital sex; the minimum standard abolishes same-sex relations and punishes publicly known infidelity with loss of citizenship. Megillus accepts the law; Cleinias defers his opinion for later, and they agree to move on.
distilled: Piety, honor, and love of spiritual beauty are the three principles restraining desire, supporting a two-tiered sexual law that ideally prohibits all extramarital sex and at minimum abolishes same-sex relations.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The triad of piety, honor, and love of soul-beauty corresponds roughly to the three parts of the soul in the Republic (rational, spirited, appetitive) and their respective motivations. The two-tiered legislative approach -- the ideal versus the achievable minimum -- exemplifies the Laws' characteristic pragmatism. The punishment of loss of civic status and being deemed a 'stranger' (xenos) reflects the gravity of citizenship in Greek political thought. Megillus's ready acceptance and Cleinias's diplomatic deferral create a moment of dramatic tension, suggesting Cleinias may have reservations rooted in Cretan customs. This passage concludes the extended discussion of sexual regulation (8.15-8.28), one of the most sustained ethical arguments in the Laws.

narrative: "We had got about as far as the establishment of the common tables, which in most places would be difficult, but in Crete no one would think of introducing any other custom. There might arise a question about the manner of them -- whether they shall be such as they are here in Crete, or such as they are in Lacedaemon, -- or is there a third kind which may be better than either of them? The answer to this question might be easily discovered, but the discovery would do no great good, for at present they are very well ordered."
refashioned: "We had reached the point of establishing the common meals -- a practice that would be difficult to introduce in most places, but that no one in Crete would dream of replacing with anything else. One might ask how these meals should be organized: should they follow the Cretan model, the Spartan model, or some third approach that improves on both? The answer to this question could probably be worked out easily enough, but the discovery would not accomplish much, since the existing arrangements are already quite satisfactory."
elegant: "We had reached the establishment of common meals -- difficult elsewhere, but natural in Crete. Whether to follow the Cretan or Spartan model, or devise a third, is an answerable question but not a pressing one, since existing arrangements are already well ordered."
summary: The Athenian transitions from the topic of sexual regulation to the practical matter of common meals, noting that while these would be difficult to establish elsewhere, they are second nature in Crete. He dismisses the question of which model to follow -- Cretan, Spartan, or a novel third option -- since the existing systems work well enough.
distilled: The common meals are already well established in Crete, and debating whether to follow the Cretan, Spartan, or a new model is unnecessary since the current arrangements are satisfactory.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The common meals (syssitia) were a central institution in both Crete and Sparta, where citizens ate together daily. In Sparta they were called phiditia. The Athenian's pragmatic acceptance of existing arrangements contrasts with the Republic's more radical communism of the guardian class (Republic 416d-417b). This passage marks a significant transition in Book 8, moving from the contentious topic of sexual regulation to the more practical concerns of agriculture and trade that will dominate the remainder of the book. The brevity of this passage signals that the Athenian considers common meals a settled question requiring no further debate.

narrative:
"Leaving the common tables, we may therefore proceed to the means of providing food. Now, in cities the means of life are gained in many ways and from divers sources, and in general from two sources, whereas our city has only one. For most of the Hellenes obtain their food from sea and land, but our citizens from land only.

And this makes the task of the legislator less difficult -- half as many laws will be enough, and much less than half; and they will be of a kind better suited to free men. For he has nothing to do with laws about shipowners and merchants and retailers and innkeepers and tax collectors and mines and moneylending and compound interest and innumerable other things -- bidding good-bye to these, he gives laws to husbandmen and shepherds and bee-keepers, and to the guardians and superintendents of their implements; and he has already legislated for greater matters, as for example, respecting marriage and the procreation and nurture of children, and for education, and the establishment of offices -- and now he must direct his laws to those who provide food and labour in preparing it."
refashioned:
"Setting aside the common meals, let us move on to the question of food production. In most cities, the means of life come from many different sources -- broadly speaking, from two: sea and land. But our city relies on land alone.

This considerably simplifies the legislator's task. He needs far fewer laws -- less than half as many -- and those laws will be better suited to free citizens. He has no need to concern himself with regulations for shipowners, merchants, retailers, innkeepers, tax collectors, mines, moneylending, compound interest, and countless other commercial matters. He can bid farewell to all of that and focus his legislation on farmers, shepherds, and beekeepers, and on those who maintain and oversee agricultural equipment. The larger matters have already been addressed: marriage, procreation, child-rearing, education, and the establishment of public offices. Now his attention must turn to those who produce food and perform the labor of preparing it."
elegant: "Our city draws its livelihood from land alone, not from sea and land like most Greek cities. This halves the legislator's work -- no need for laws on shipping, commerce, banking, mining, or interest. He legislates only for farmers, shepherds, and beekeepers. Marriage, education, and governance have been addressed; now we must attend to food production."
summary: The Athenian transitions to agricultural legislation, noting that the proposed city's land-only economy greatly simplifies the legislator's task. He can ignore the entire commercial apparatus of maritime cities -- shipping, trade, banking, mining -- and focus entirely on farmers, shepherds, and beekeepers. The major social institutions have already been established; now attention turns to food production.
distilled: A land-only economy simplifies legislation enormously, freeing the lawmaker from commercial regulation to focus exclusively on agriculture, shepherding, and food production.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The exclusion of maritime commerce reflects Plato's deep suspicion of trade and seafaring as sources of moral corruption -- cf. Laws 704d-707d, where he argues that coastal cities are corrupted by commerce. The list of excluded activities (shipowners, merchants, moneylending, compound interest) reads like a catalog of everything Plato distrusted about Athenian economic life. The positive focus on husbandmen, shepherds, and beekeepers reflects the agrarian ideal common to both Platonic and later Roman political thought. The claim that agricultural laws are 'better suited to free men' implies that commercial activity is inherently servile -- a widespread aristocratic Greek attitude. This passage begins the agricultural legislation section (8.30-8.46) that concludes Book 8.

narrative:
"Let us first of all, then, have a class of laws which shall be called the laws of husbandmen. And let the first of them be the law of Zeus, the god of boundaries. Let no one shift the boundary line either of a fellow-citizen who is a neighbour, or, if he dwells at the extremity of the land, of any stranger who is conterminous with him, considering that this is truly 'to move the immovable,' and every one should be more willing to move the largest rock which is not a landmark, than the least stone which is the sworn mark of friendship and hatred between neighbours; for Zeus, the god of kindred, is the witness of the citizen, and Zeus, the god of strangers, of the stranger, and when aroused, terrible are the wars which they stir up.

He who obeys the law will never know the fatal consequences of disobedience, but he who despises the law shall be liable to a double penalty, the first coming from the Gods, and the second from the law. For let no one wilfully remove the boundaries of his neighbour's land, and if any one does, let him who will inform the landowners, and let them bring him into court, and if he be convicted of re-dividing the land by stealth or by force, let the court determine what he ought to suffer or pay."
refashioned:
"Let us begin, then, with a class of laws that we shall call the laws of husbandmen. The first shall be the law of Zeus, god of boundaries: no one shall move a boundary marker, whether the neighbor is a fellow citizen or a foreigner living at the edge of the territory. To move a boundary stone is truly 'to move the unmovable.' A person should sooner move the largest boulder that is not a landmark than the smallest stone that marks the sworn boundary between neighbors. For Zeus in his aspect as god of kinship watches over citizens, and Zeus as god of strangers watches over foreigners -- and the wars these gods stir up when provoked are terrible.

Those who obey this law will never experience the dreadful consequences of violating it. But anyone who defies it faces a double penalty: first from the gods, and second from the law. No one shall willfully remove a neighbor's boundary markers. If anyone does, let any willing person report it to the landowners, who shall bring the offender to court. If convicted of redividing land by stealth or force, the court shall determine the appropriate punishment or fine."
elegant:
"The first law of husbandmen: the law of Zeus, god of boundaries. No one shall move a boundary stone -- to do so is to move the unmovable. Better to move the largest boulder than the smallest landmark, for Zeus watches over boundaries between both citizens and foreigners, and the wars he provokes are terrible.

Violators face a double penalty -- from the gods and from the law. Anyone who removes a neighbor's boundary by stealth or force shall be brought to court and punished as the court sees fit."
summary: The Athenian establishes the first agricultural law under the protection of Zeus, god of boundaries: boundary stones are sacred and must never be moved. He invokes the religious sanction of Zeus in his dual aspects as protector of citizens and strangers, warning that violators face punishment from both divine and human law. Offenders who redivide land by stealth or force shall be tried and punished by the court.
distilled: Boundary stones are sacred under Zeus's protection, and anyone who moves them faces both divine punishment and legal prosecution.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The sacredness of boundary stones (horoi) was deeply embedded in Greek religious and legal culture. Zeus Horios (Zeus of Boundaries) was a well-attested cult figure. The phrase 'to move the immovable' (ta akineta kinein) was proverbial in Greek, echoing a Pythagorean maxim. The dual aspect of Zeus as protector of both citizens (Zeus Homognios/Phratrios) and strangers (Zeus Xenios) is well attested in Greek religion -- cf. Odyssey 9.270-271 on Zeus Xenios. The double penalty (divine and legal) reflects the Laws' consistent fusion of religious and civil authority. Roman law later developed similar protections for boundary markers (terminus), suggesting a shared Mediterranean legal heritage.

narrative:
"In the next place, many small injuries done by neighbours to one another, through their multiplication, may cause a weight of enmity, and make neighbourhood a very disagreeable and bitter thing. Wherefore a man ought to be very careful of committing any offence against his neighbour, and especially of encroaching on his neighbour's land; for any man may easily do harm, but not every man can do good to another.

He who encroaches on his neighbour's land, and transgresses his boundaries, shall make good the damage, and, to cure him of his impudence and also of his meanness, he shall pay a double penalty to the injured party. Of these and the like matters the wardens of the country shall take cognizance, and be the judges of them and assessors of the damage; in the more important cases, as has been already said, the whole number of them belonging to any one of the twelve divisions shall decide, and in the lesser cases the commanders: or, again, if any one pastures his cattle on his neighbour's land, they shall see the injury, and adjudge the penalty."
refashioned:
"Furthermore, many small offenses between neighbors, when they accumulate, can create a crushing weight of enmity and make neighborhood life deeply unpleasant and bitter. Therefore, every person should take great care not to commit any offense against a neighbor, especially encroachment on their land. It is easy for anyone to do harm, but not everyone can do good.

Anyone who encroaches on a neighbor's land and crosses their boundaries must compensate for the damage and, as a cure for both their arrogance and their pettiness, pay the injured party double the amount of the loss. The wardens of the countryside shall have jurisdiction over such cases and shall serve as both judges and assessors of damages. In more significant disputes, all the wardens of the relevant district among the twelve divisions shall decide; in lesser cases, the local commanders shall judge. If anyone grazes their cattle on a neighbor's land, the wardens shall assess the harm and determine the penalty."
elegant:
"Many small neighborly offenses, multiplied over time, breed deep enmity. Citizens must take care, especially regarding land encroachment -- it is easy to do harm, but not easy to do good.

Encroachers must repair the damage and pay double as a cure for their arrogance and pettiness. Country wardens judge these matters -- all of them for major cases, local commanders for minor ones, including unauthorized cattle grazing."
summary: The Athenian warns that accumulated small injuries between neighbors create bitter enmity and prescribes double penalties for land encroachment to cure both arrogance and meanness. The wardens of the countryside serve as judges, with the full body deciding important cases and local commanders handling minor ones, including unauthorized cattle grazing.
distilled: Small neighborly offenses breed lasting enmity, so land encroachment is punished with double penalties, and country wardens adjudicate all such disputes.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The observation that 'any man may easily do harm, but not every man can do good' is a memorable aphorism reflecting a pessimistic strand in Plato's anthropology. The double penalty for encroachment serves both compensatory and punitive functions -- 'cure of impudence and meanness' -- anticipating modern discussions of punitive damages. The tiered judicial system (full wardens for major cases, commanders for minor) reflects the Laws' detailed institutional design. Cattle-grazing disputes were common in ancient Mediterranean agricultural communities and feature in the legal codes of many ancient Near Eastern societies as well.

narrative:
"And if any one, by decoying the bees, gets possession of another's swarms, and draws them to himself by making noises, he shall pay the damage; or if anyone sets fire to his own wood and takes no care of his neighbour's property, he shall be fined at the discretion of the magistrates.

And if in planting he does not leave a fair distance between his own and his neighbour's land, he shall be punished, in accordance with the enactments of many lawgivers, which we may use, not deeming it necessary that the great legislator of our state should determine all the trifles which might be decided by any body; for example, husbandmen have had of old excellent laws about waters, and there is no reason why we should propose to divert their course: who likes may draw water from the fountain-head of the common stream on to his own land, if he do not cut off the spring which clearly belongs to some other owner; and he may take the water in any direction which he pleases, except through a house or temple or sepulchre, but he must be careful to do no harm beyond the channel."
refashioned:
"If anyone lures another's bees away by making noises and thereby takes possession of their swarms, he must pay for the damage. If anyone sets fire to his own timber without safeguarding his neighbor's property, the magistrates shall fine him at their discretion.

If someone plants too close to a neighbor's boundary without leaving adequate space, he shall be punished according to the widely established laws of many legislators. We can adopt these existing regulations without requiring the chief legislator of our state to determine every minor detail that could be decided by anyone.

For example, farmers have long had excellent customary laws regarding water rights, and there is no reason to overturn them. Anyone may draw water from the source of a common stream onto his own land, provided he does not cut off a spring that clearly belongs to someone else. He may channel the water in any direction he chooses, except through a house, temple, or burial ground, and he must take care to cause no damage beyond the irrigation channel."
elegant:
"Decoying bees, negligent burning, and planting too close to a boundary are all punishable offenses. But the great legislator need not determine every minor detail -- existing customary laws, such as those governing water rights, serve well enough.

Anyone may draw from a common stream onto their land without cutting off another's spring, and may channel water freely except through houses, temples, or tombs, taking care to cause no collateral damage."
summary: The Athenian covers specific agricultural offenses -- bee theft, negligent burning, and encroachment through planting -- then argues that the chief legislator need not legislate every detail. Existing customary laws, particularly regarding water rights, are adequate. He outlines water-use rights: anyone may draw from common streams without cutting off others' springs, and may channel water freely except through buildings, temples, or burial grounds.
distilled: Minor agricultural offenses follow existing customary laws, and water may be drawn freely from common streams provided no one's spring is diverted and no sacred or private structures are damaged.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The bee-decoying law reflects the economic importance of honey in ancient Greece, where it served as the primary sweetener. The principle that the great legislator should not legislate 'trifles' is a significant statement about legislative delegation and the limits of top-down legal design. Water rights were among the most contentious issues in ancient Mediterranean agriculture, and the Laws' provisions here parallel Solon's well-digging laws (Plutarch, Life of Solon 23). The protection of houses, temples, and sepulchres from water channels reflects the sacred status of all three in Greek culture. The practical, customary approach to agricultural law in this section contrasts with the more theoretical approach to sexual legislation in 8.15-8.28.

narrative:
"And if there be in any place a natural dryness of the earth, which keeps in the rain from heaven, and causes a deficiency in the supply of water, let him dig down on his own land as far as the clay, and if at this depth he finds no water, let him obtain water from his neighbours, as much as is required for his servants' drinking, and if his neighbours, too, are limited in their supply, let him have a fixed measure, which shall be determined by the wardens of the country. This he shall receive each day, and on these terms have a share of his neighbours' water.

If there be heavy rain, and one of those on the lower ground injures some tiller of the upper ground, or some one who has a common wall, by refusing to give the man outlet for water; or, again, if some one living on the higher ground recklessly lets off the water on his lower neighbour, and they cannot come to terms with one another, let him who will call in a warden of the city, if he be in the city, or if he be in the country, a warden of the country, and let him obtain a decision determining what each of them is to do. And he who will not abide by the decision shall suffer for his malignant and morose temper, and pay a fine to the injured party, equivalent to double the value of the injury, because he was unwilling to submit to the magistrates."
refashioned:
"If any area suffers from naturally dry soil that absorbs the rainfall and creates a water shortage, the landowner may dig down on his own property as far as the clay layer. If he finds no water at that depth, he may obtain water from his neighbors -- enough for his servants' drinking needs. If the neighbors themselves have limited water, a fixed daily ration shall be established by the wardens of the countryside. On these terms he shall receive his share of his neighbors' water each day.

In cases of heavy rain: if someone on lower ground harms an uphill farmer or a person sharing a common wall by refusing to allow drainage, or if someone on higher ground carelessly lets water flood down onto a lower neighbor, and the parties cannot resolve the dispute themselves, either party may summon a warden -- a warden of the city if the dispute is in the city, or a warden of the country if it is in the countryside. The warden shall determine what each party must do. Anyone who refuses to comply with the decision shall be punished for his obstinate and contentious character and shall pay the injured party a fine equal to double the value of the harm caused, for his refusal to submit to the authority of the magistrates."
elegant:
"If natural dryness causes water shortage, a landowner may dig to the clay on his own property. If no water is found, he may receive a daily ration from neighbors, fixed by the country wardens.

Drainage disputes between uphill and downhill neighbors, if unresolvable privately, shall be decided by the appropriate warden. Anyone who refuses the ruling pays double damages for their obstinacy."
summary: The Athenian provides detailed water-rights legislation for drought conditions, allowing landowners to dig for water on their own property and, if unsuccessful, to receive regulated daily rations from neighbors. For flood-water drainage disputes between uphill and downhill neighbors that cannot be privately resolved, wardens shall arbitrate, with double penalties for anyone who refuses to comply with the ruling.
distilled: Landowners may dig for water on their property and receive rationed neighbor water in drought, while drainage disputes are resolved by wardens with double penalties for noncompliance.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage provides remarkably detailed hydrological legislation, reflecting the critical importance of water management in Crete and throughout the ancient Mediterranean. The provision for digging 'as far as the clay' shows practical knowledge of aquifer structures. The distinction between city and country wardens reflects the Laws' dual administrative system. The double penalty for refusing a magistrate's decision is noteworthy -- it punishes not the original harm but the contempt for legal authority, reflecting the Laws' emphasis on obedience to legitimate decisions. The characterization of the noncompliant person as having a 'malignant and morose temper' is psychologically specific and suggests that the Laws views obstructionism as a character flaw rather than merely a legal violation.

narrative:
"Now the participation of fruits shall be ordered on this wise. The goddess of Autumn has two gracious gifts: one, the joy of Dionysus which is not treasured up; the other, which nature intends to be stored. Let this be the law, then, concerning the fruits of autumn:

He who tastes the common or storing fruits of autumn, whether grapes or figs, before the season of vintage which coincides with Arcturus, either on his own land or on that of others -- let him pay fifty drachmae, which shall be sacred to Dionysus, if he pluck them from his own land; and if from his neighbour's land, a mina, and if from any others', two-thirds of a mina.

And he who would gather the 'choice' grapes or the 'choice' figs, as they are now termed, if he take them off his own land, let him pluck them how and when he likes; but if he take them from the ground of others without their leave, let him in that case be always punished in accordance with the law which ordains that he should not move what he has not laid down.

And if a slave touches any fruit of this sort, without the consent of the owner of the land, he shall be beaten with as many blows as there are grapes on the bunch, or figs on the fig-tree.

Let a metic purchase the 'choice' autumnal fruit, and then, if he pleases, he may gather it; but if a stranger is passing along the road, and desires to eat, let him take of the 'choice' grapes for himself and a single follower without payment, as a tribute of hospitality."
refashioned:
"The sharing of fruits shall be regulated as follows. The goddess of Autumn bestows two gifts: one is the joy of Dionysus -- the fresh fruit that is consumed immediately and not preserved; the other is the fruit that nature intends for storage.

The law concerning autumn fruits shall be this: anyone who picks the common or storable fruits -- grapes or figs -- before the vintage season (which coincides with the rising of Arcturus), whether from his own land or another's, shall be fined. Fifty drachmae, dedicated to Dionysus, if he picks from his own land; one mina if from a neighbor's land; and two-thirds of a mina from anyone else's.

As for the 'choice' varieties of grapes and figs, as they are called: a citizen may pick them from his own land whenever and however he likes. But if he picks them from another's land without permission, he shall always be punished according to the principle that one should not take what one did not plant.

If a slave picks any such fruit without the landowner's consent, he shall receive as many blows as there are grapes on the cluster or figs on the tree.

A resident foreigner may purchase the 'choice' autumn fruit, after which he may pick it freely. But a passing stranger who wants to eat may help himself to 'choice' grapes for himself and one companion, free of charge, as a tribute of hospitality."
elegant:
"Autumn gives two gifts: fresh fruit for immediate enjoyment (Dionysus's gift) and fruit for storage. Picking storable fruit before the vintage season brings fines -- fifty drachmae from one's own land, more from others'. 'Choice' fruits may be freely picked from one's own land, but not from another's without permission.

Slaves who steal fruit receive one blow per grape or fig. Resident foreigners may buy 'choice' fruit; passing strangers may take grapes for two without payment, as a tribute of hospitality."
summary: The Athenian establishes detailed harvest laws distinguishing between storable fruits (protected by fines for premature picking) and 'choice' table fruits (freely available from one's own land). Unauthorized fruit-taking from others' property is penalized at escalating rates. Slaves face corporal punishment proportional to the quantity stolen. Resident foreigners must purchase fruit, but passing travelers may take grapes for two as a customary act of hospitality.
distilled: Harvest laws protect storable fruits from premature picking and others' fruit from unauthorized taking, while hospitality customs allow passing strangers to take a small amount of fresh fruit freely.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The 'goddess of Autumn' (Opora) was a minor deity associated with the fruit harvest. The rising of Arcturus (mid-September) marked the traditional beginning of the grape and fig harvest in Greece. The distinction between 'common/storing' and 'choice' fruit reflects actual ancient agricultural categories. The fine dedicated to Dionysus connects harvest regulation to religious observance. The slave punishment (one blow per grape/fig) is hyperbolically severe and may be partly rhetorical. The hospitality provision for passing strangers reflects the deep Greek value of xenia (guest-friendship) -- cf. Odyssey, passim, and Laws 729e on the protection of strangers. The gradation of penalties (own land, neighbor's, others') creates a nuanced legal framework.

narrative:
"The law however forbids strangers from sharing in the sort which is not used for eating; and if any one, whether he be master or slave, takes of them in ignorance, let the slave be beaten, and the freeman dismissed with admonitions, and instructed to take of the other autumnal fruits which are unfit for making raisins and wine, or for laying by as dried figs.

As to pears, and apples, and pomegranates, and similar fruits, there shall be no disgrace in taking them secretly; but he who is caught, if he be of less than thirty years of age, shall be struck and beaten off, but not wounded; and no freeman shall have any right of satisfaction for such blows. Of these fruits the stranger may partake, just as he may of the fruits of autumn.

And if an elder, who is more than thirty years of age, eat of them on the spot, let him, like the stranger, be allowed to partake of all such fruits, but he must carry away nothing. If, however, he will not obey the law, let him run risk of failing in the competition of virtue, in case any one takes notice of his actions before the judges at the time."
refashioned:
"However, strangers are forbidden from taking the fruit meant for storage. If anyone, master or slave, takes such fruit out of ignorance, the slave shall be beaten and the free person shall be warned and instructed to take only those autumn fruits that are not suitable for making raisins, wine, or dried figs.

Regarding pears, apples, pomegranates, and similar fruits: there is no disgrace in taking them secretly. But anyone under thirty who is caught shall be struck and driven off -- without being wounded -- and no free person may claim legal satisfaction for such blows. Strangers may partake of these fruits, just as they may of the autumn harvest fruits.

As for someone over thirty: he may eat such fruits on the spot, just like a stranger, but he must not carry any away. If he refuses to obey this rule, he risks failing in the competition of virtue, should anyone report his behavior to the judges."
elegant:
"Strangers may not take storable fruit; ignorant offenders are warned or beaten according to status. Pears, apples, and pomegranates may be secretly taken without disgrace, though those under thirty who are caught shall be struck but not wounded.

Those over thirty may eat such fruit on the spot like strangers but may carry nothing away. Disobedience risks failing in the competition of virtue."
summary: The Athenian clarifies that strangers may not take storable fruit, and sets differentiated rules for other fruits like pears, apples, and pomegranates. Secret taking carries no shame, but those under thirty who are caught may be physically driven off. Those over thirty may eat on the spot but take nothing away. The ultimate sanction for disobedience is reputational: risking failure in the competition of virtue.
distilled: Casual fruit-taking is tolerated with age-graded rules, but storable fruit is protected, and persistent disobedience risks a citizen's standing in the competition of virtue.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The 'competition of virtue' (hamilla aretes) refers to the civic evaluation system described in Laws 6.762b-c, where citizens are assessed and ranked for moral conduct -- a powerful social incentive unique to the Laws' proposed city. The age distinction (under/over thirty) reflects Greek assumptions about the different social expectations appropriate to different life stages -- cf. the age requirements for various offices throughout the Laws. The toleration of secret fruit-taking (no 'disgrace') creates an interesting legal gray zone where the act is not criminalized but getting caught has consequences. The differentiation between storable and fresh fruit reflects the economic reality that dried figs, raisins, and wine were valuable trade commodities, while fresh fruit was perishable and less economically significant.

narrative:
"Water is the greatest element of nutrition in gardens, but is easily polluted. You cannot poison the soil, or the sun, or the air, which are other elements of nutrition in plants, or divert them, or steal them; but all these things may very likely happen in regard to water, which must therefore be protected by law.

And let this be the law: If any one intentionally pollutes the water of another, whether the water of a spring, or collected in reservoirs, either by poisonous substances, or by digging or by theft, let the injured party bring the cause before the wardens of the city, and claim in writing the value of the loss; if the accused be found guilty of injuring the water by deleterious substances, let him not only pay damages, but purify the stream or the cistern which contains the water, in such manner as the laws of the interpreters order the purification to be made by the offender in each case."
refashioned:
"Water is the most vital element for growing gardens, but it is also the most easily polluted. You cannot poison the soil, the sun, or the air -- the other essential elements for plant growth -- nor can you divert or steal them. But all of these things can and do happen with water. Therefore, water must be protected by law.

The law shall be this: if anyone intentionally pollutes another person's water supply -- whether a spring or a reservoir -- by introducing poisonous substances, by digging to divert it, or by stealing it, the injured party shall bring the case before the wardens of the city and submit a written claim for the value of the loss. If the accused is found guilty of contaminating the water with harmful substances, he must not only pay damages but must also personally purify the stream or cistern, following the purification procedures prescribed by the religious interpreters for each specific case."
elegant:
"Water is the most vital and most vulnerable element in agriculture. Unlike soil, sun, and air, it can be poisoned, diverted, or stolen, and therefore requires legal protection.

Anyone who intentionally pollutes another's water -- by poison, diversion, or theft -- must pay damages and personally purify the contaminated source according to the religious interpreters' prescribed methods."
summary: The Athenian identifies water as uniquely vulnerable among agricultural necessities because it alone can be poisoned, diverted, or stolen. He establishes that intentional water pollution shall be prosecuted before the city wardens, with the guilty party required both to pay damages and to personally purify the contaminated water source according to religious ritual.
distilled: Water, uniquely vulnerable to pollution, diversion, and theft among agricultural necessities, requires legal protection with penalties including both damages and mandatory religious purification.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The ecological insight that water is uniquely susceptible to human interference among the four elements of plant nutrition (soil, sun, air, water) is remarkably perceptive. The combination of financial penalties (damages) and religious obligation (purification) reflects the Laws' fusion of secular and sacred authority. The role of the 'interpreters' (exegetai) in prescribing purification rituals connects to the established Athenian institution of religious interpreters who advised on matters of ritual pollution -- cf. Laws 759c-d. Water pollution laws have parallels in other ancient legal codes, including the Code of Hammurabi. The requirement for written claims shows the Laws' reliance on documented legal proceedings.

narrative:
"With respect to the gathering in of the fruits of the soil, let a man, if he pleases, carry his own fruits through any place in which he either does no harm to any one, or himself gains three times as much as his neighbour loses. Now of these things the magistrates should be cognisant, as of all other things in which a man intentionally does injury to another or to the property of another, by fraud or force, in the use which he makes of his own property.

All these matters a man should lay before the magistrates, and receive damages, supposing the injury to be not more than three minae; or if he have a charge against another which involves a larger amount, let him bring his suit into the public courts and have the evil-doer punished.

But if any of the magistrates appear to adjudge the penalties which he imposes in an unjust spirit, let him be liable to pay double to the injured party. Any one may bring the offences of magistrates, in any particular case, before the public courts.

There are innumerable little matters relating to the modes of punishment, and applications for suits, and summonses and the witnesses to summonses -- for example, whether two witnesses should be required for a summons, or how many -- and all such details, which cannot be omitted in legislation, but are beneath the wisdom of an aged legislator. These lesser matters, as they indeed are in comparison with the greater ones, let a younger generation regulate by law, after the patterns which have preceded, and according to their own experience of the usefulness and necessity of such laws; and when they are duly regulated let there be no alteration, but let the citizens live in the observance of them."
refashioned:
"Regarding the harvest: a person may transport his own produce through any route, provided he either causes no harm to anyone or gains three times more than his neighbor loses from the passage. Magistrates should oversee these matters, as well as all cases where someone intentionally harms another person or their property -- whether by fraud or force -- through the use of his own property.

Claims involving three minae or less shall be brought before the magistrates for resolution. Claims exceeding that amount shall be taken to the public courts for prosecution and punishment of the offender.

If any magistrate is found to have rendered judgment in an unjust spirit, he shall be liable to pay double the amount to the injured party. Any citizen may bring charges of judicial misconduct against a magistrate before the public courts.

There are countless procedural details -- methods of punishment, procedures for filing suits, rules for summonses, the number of witnesses required for a summons, and similar matters -- that legislation cannot omit but that are beneath the concern of an elderly legislator. Let a younger generation establish these procedural laws, following the patterns already laid down and drawing on their own practical experience of what is useful and necessary. Once these regulations are properly established, they should remain unchanged, and citizens should live by them."
elegant:
"A person may transport his produce through any route where the benefit outweighs the harm threefold. Magistrates handle disputes up to three minae; larger claims go to public courts. Magistrates who judge unjustly pay double to the injured party and may be prosecuted.

Countless procedural details -- summonses, witness requirements, filing procedures -- are necessary but beneath an aged legislator. Let the younger generation codify these, following established principles, and once settled, keep them fixed."
summary: The Athenian establishes rules for transporting produce, a tiered judicial system for agricultural disputes (magistrates for small claims, public courts for larger ones), and accountability for unjust magistrates who face double penalties. He then delegates procedural details to future younger legislators, acknowledging that such minutiae are necessary but unworthy of an elderly lawgiver's attention.
distilled: Agricultural disputes are handled by magistrates for small claims and public courts for larger ones, with unjust magistrates facing double penalties, while procedural details are delegated to younger legislators.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The three-to-one benefit-to-harm ratio for produce transport is a notable early instance of cost-benefit analysis in law. The double penalty for unjust magistrates demonstrates the Laws' emphasis on judicial accountability -- a theme developed extensively in Laws 12.945b-948b. The principle that citizens may bring charges against magistrates reflects a democratic element in the otherwise mixed constitution. The delegation of procedural details to younger legislators is both practical and philosophically interesting, as it acknowledges the limits of top-down legislative design and the need for experiential wisdom. The instruction to keep these procedural laws fixed once established reflects the Laws' general conservatism about legal change -- cf. Laws 7.797a-798d on the danger of innovation.

narrative:
"Now of artisans, let the regulations be as follows: In the first place, let no citizen or servant of a citizen be occupied in handicraft arts; for he who is to secure and preserve the public order of the state, has an art which requires much study and many kinds of knowledge, and does not admit of being made a secondary occupation; and hardly any human being is capable of pursuing two professions or two arts rightly, or of practising one art himself, and superintending some one else who is practising another.

Let this, then, be our first principle in the state: No one who is a smith shall also be a carpenter, and if he be a carpenter, he shall not superintend the smith's art rather than his own, under the pretext that in superintending many servants who are working for him, he is likely to superintend them better, because more revenue will accrue to him from them than from his own art; but let every man in the state have one art, and get his living by that.

Let the wardens of the city labour to maintain this law, and if any citizen incline to any other art than the study of virtue, let them punish him with disgrace and infamy, until they bring him back into his own right course; and if any stranger profess two arts, let them chastise him with bonds and money penalties, and expulsion from the state, until they compel him to be one only and not many."
refashioned:
"Regarding artisans, the regulations shall be as follows. First, no citizen or citizen's servant shall practice a manual trade. The art of maintaining public order requires extensive study and diverse knowledge, and cannot be treated as a side occupation. Almost no human being can properly pursue two professions, or practice one trade while supervising someone else practicing another.

Our fundamental principle: no smith shall also be a carpenter, and no carpenter shall supervise a smithy instead of his own workshop, even under the excuse that managing many workers in a different trade generates more revenue than his own craft. Every person in the state shall have one trade and earn his living from that alone.

The wardens of the city shall enforce this law. If any citizen turns to any occupation other than the pursuit of virtue, they shall punish him with public disgrace and dishonor until he returns to his proper course. If any foreigner practices two trades, he shall be punished with imprisonment, fines, and expulsion from the state until he is compelled to practice one trade only, not several."
elegant:
"No citizen shall practice a manual trade -- civic duty is itself a full-time art requiring extensive study. Even among artisans, no one shall practice two trades: a smith must not also be a carpenter, nor supervise another's trade for profit.

Citizens who stray from the pursuit of virtue face public disgrace. Foreigners practicing two trades face imprisonment, fines, and expulsion. Every person shall have one art and live by it alone."
summary: The Athenian establishes that citizens are barred from manual trades because civic duty is itself a full-time profession. Among artisans, strict specialization is required: each person must practice a single trade and earn their living from it alone. Citizens who neglect virtue for other pursuits face public disgrace, while foreigners who practice multiple trades face imprisonment, fines, and banishment.
distilled: Citizens must devote themselves entirely to virtue and civic duty rather than manual trades, and every artisan must practice one craft only, with severe penalties for those who diversify.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The prohibition on citizens practicing trades reflects the widespread Greek aristocratic disdain for banausic (manual) labor -- cf. Xenophon, Oeconomicus 4.2-3 and Aristotle, Politics 1278a. The principle of one person, one trade echoes Republic 370b-374d, where specialization is the founding principle of the just city. The argument that supervising others' work for profit should not override one's own craft anticipates concerns about managerial capitalism. The distinction between citizen penalties (disgrace) and foreigner penalties (physical, financial, and expulsion) reflects the different legal statuses in Greek city-states. The characterization of the citizen's proper art as 'the study of virtue' is the passage's most significant philosophical claim -- it redefines citizenship itself as a vocation.

narrative:
"But as touching payments for hire, and contracts of work, or in case any one does wrong to any of the citizens or they do wrong to any other, up to fifty drachmae, let the wardens of the city decide the case; but if a greater amount be involved, then let the public courts decide according to law.

Let no one pay any duty either on the importation or exportation of goods; and as to frankincense and similar perfumes, used in the service of the Gods, which come from abroad, and purple and other dyes which are not produced in the country, or the materials of any art which have to be imported, and which are not necessary -- no one should import them; nor again, should any one export anything which is wanted in the country.

Of all these things let there be inspectors and superintendents, taken from the guardians of the law; and they shall be the twelve next in order to the five seniors.

Concerning arms, and all implements which are for military purposes, if there be need of introducing any art, or plant, or metal, or chains of any kind, or animals for use in war, let the commanders of the horse and the generals have authority over their importation and exportation; the city shall send them out and also receive them, and the guardians of the law shall make fit and proper laws about them. But let there be no retail trade for the sake of money-making, either in these or any other articles, in the city or country at all."
refashioned:
"Regarding wages, work contracts, and civil wrongs involving amounts up to fifty drachmae: the wardens of the city shall decide these cases. For amounts exceeding fifty drachmae, the public courts shall decide according to law.

No customs duties shall be imposed on either imports or exports. However, certain goods shall not be imported at all: frankincense and similar religious perfumes from abroad, purple and other dyes not produced domestically, and the materials for any art that must be imported but are not essential. Likewise, nothing that is needed within the country shall be exported.

Inspectors and superintendents for these trade regulations shall be drawn from the guardians of the law -- specifically, the twelve next in seniority after the five senior guardians.

For military materiel -- including any craft, plant, metal, chains, or animals needed for warfare -- the cavalry commanders and generals shall have authority over importation and exportation. The city itself shall conduct these transactions, and the guardians of the law shall establish appropriate regulations.
